Ayah

إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُّبِينًا (١)
Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory, (1)

Content of Surah al-Fath

As its name suggests, this Surah is a herald of victory and triumph—victory over the enemies of Islam, a striking and decisive victory (whether it refers to the Conquest of Makkah, the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, the Conquest of Khaybar, or victory in an absolute sense).

To understand the content of this Surah, it is first necessary to know that it was revealed after the event of "Hudaybiyyah" in the sixth year of the Hijrah.

To explain: In the sixth year of the Hijrah, the Prophet of Islam ص decided to set out toward Makkah accompanied by the Muhajirin, the Ansar, and other Muslims to perform the "Umrah" pilgrimage. He had previously informed the Muslims, saying: "I saw in a dream that I, along with my companions, entered the Masjid al-Haram and were engaged in the rituals of Umrah."

The Muslims donned the Ihram at Dhu al-Hulayfah near Madinah and set out with a large number of camels for sacrifice.

The manner of the Prophet’s ص movement clearly showed that he had no goal other than performing this great act of worship. Eventually, the Prophet ص entered the land of "Hudaybiyyah" (Hudaybiyyah was a village near Makkah, about 20 kilometers away).

However, at this point, the Quraysh became aware and blocked the Prophet’s ص path, preventing him from entering Makkah. In doing so, they essentially trampled upon all the traditions they held regarding the security of the pilgrims of the House of Allah during the sacred months. They believed that during the sacred months (including the month of Dhu al-Qi'dah, in which the Prophet intended to perform Umrah), and especially while in the state of Ihram, no one should be obstructed. Even if someone saw the murderer of their own father during these days and ceremonies, they would not confront him at all.

A detailed series of events occurred here, leading to the signing of a peace treaty between the Prophet ص and the polytheists of Makkah known as the "Treaty of Hudaybiyyah," which we will discuss later. Regardless, that year they prevented the Prophet ص from entering Makkah. Consequently, the Prophet ص commanded his companions to sacrifice their camels right there, shave their heads, exit the state of Ihram, and return to Madinah.

At this moment, a storm of grief and sorrow enveloped the Muslims, and a mass of distress—and at times, doubt and hesitation—overcame those with weak faith.

As the Prophet ص was returning from Hudaybiyyah toward Madinah, his mount became heavy and stopped moving. At that very moment, his blessed countenance became radiant with unprecedented joy and happiness, and he said: "Just now, the verses of Surah al-Fath were revealed to me." [^1] From this, the specific atmosphere governing this Surah becomes fully apparent.

In a brief overview, it can be said that this Surah consists of seven sections:

  1. The Surah begins with the glad tidings of victory, and its concluding verses are also related to this matter, emphasizing the fulfillment of the Prophet’s ص dream regarding entering Makkah and performing the Umrah rituals.

  2. Another section of the Surah recounts the events related to the "Treaty of Hudaybiyyah," the "descent of Sakinah" (tranquility) upon the hearts of the believers, and the matter of the "Pledge of Ridwan" (Bay'at al-Ridwan).

  3. Another section speaks of the status of the Prophet and his lofty objective.

[^1]: Summarized from Tafsir al-Qummi, Majma' al-Bayan, and Fi Zilal.

  1. Another part unveils the obstructions caused by the hypocrites and examples of their hollow excuses for not participating in the field of Jihad.

  2. Another section reflects some of the improper demands made by the hypocrites.

  3. It then introduces those who are excused from participating in the field of Jihad.

  4. Finally, one section speaks of the characteristics of the followers of the Prophet’s ص school of thought and their specific attributes.

On the whole, the verses of this Surah are exceptionally sensitive and decisive, and they are particularly inspiring for Muslims today in the face of the various events confronting Islamic societies.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah al-Fath

Remarkable narrations regarding this Surah are found in Islamic sources:

In a Hadith from Anas, he says: "When we were returning from Hudaybiyyah, while the polytheists had prevented us from entering Makkah and performing the Umrah rituals, we were deeply immersed in sorrow and grief. Suddenly, Allah revealed the verse: 'إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ فَتْحاً مُبِيناً' (Indeed, We have granted you a clear victory)."

The Prophet ص said:

"لقد انزلت على آية هى احب الى من الدنيا كلها"

"A verse has been revealed to me that is more beloved to me than the entire world." (In some narrations, it is also stated: "A Surah has been revealed to me...") [^1] Abdullah ibn Mas'ud says: "Upon the return from Hudaybiyyah, when 'إِنَّا فَتَحْنا...' was revealed to the Prophet ص, he became so filled with joy that only Allah knows." [^1] In another Hadith from the Prophet ص, it is stated:

"من قرأها فكانما شهد مع محمد (ص) فتح مكه،"

And in another narration:

"فكانما كان مع من بايع محمدا تحت الشجرة:"

"Whoever recites this Surah is like one who was in the service of the Prophet ص and in his army during the Conquest of Makkah." And in another narration: "It is as if he were among those who pledged allegiance to Muhammad ص under the tree at Hudaybiyyah." [^2] Finally, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"حصنوا اموالكم و نسائكم و ما ملكت ايمانكم من التلف بقرائة 'إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ' فانه اذا كان ممن يدمن قراءتها نادى مناد يوم القيامة حتى يسمع الخلائق، انت من عبادى المخلصين، الحقوه بالصالحين من عبادى، و ادخلوه جنات النعيم، و اسقوه من رحيق مختوم بمزاج الكافور!:"

"Protect your wealth, your wives, and your possessions from loss by reciting 'إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ'. For if one is among those who recite it constantly, a caller will cry out on the Day of Resurrection such that all creation hears: 'You are among My sincere servants; join him with My righteous servants, admit him into the Gardens of Delight, and give him to drink of the Sealed Nectar mixed with camphor!'" [^3] It goes without saying that all this virtue and honor is not achieved through recitation devoid of reflection and action; rather, the primary goal of recitation is to align one's actions and character with the content of these verses.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 108. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 108. [^3]: Thawab al-A'mal, as cited in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 46.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ فَتْحاً مُبِيناً (1)

Commentary

The Clear Victory!

In the first verse of this Surah, a great glad tiding is given to the Prophet ص—a tiding which, according to some narrations, was more beloved to the Prophet ص than the entire world. He says: "Indeed, We have granted you a clear and manifest victory" (إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ فَتْحاً مُبِيناً).

A striking victory whose effects have been and will be manifest in the short and long term in the progress of Islam and the lives of Muslims—a victory that was rare or unparalleled throughout the history of Islam.

Here, a great discussion and debate has arisen among the commentators: which victory is meant by this "victory"? Most commentators consider it a reference to the great victory that the Muslims gained from the "Treaty of Hudaybiyyah." [^1] Some have considered it a reference to the "Conquest of Makkah."

While some consider it to refer to the "Conquest of Khaybar."

And some have considered it a reference to the victory of Islam over all enemies through the power of logic and the superiority of clear proofs and miracles.

Finally, some consider it a reference to the opening of the secrets of knowledge for the Prophet ص.

However, there are numerous clues that favor the matter of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. But to clarify the commentary of these verses, we must first provide a summary of the story of Hudaybiyyah, which serves as the context of revelation (shan al-nuzul).

The Story of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah

In the sixth year of the Hijrah, in the month of Dhu al-Qi'dah, the Holy Prophet ص set out toward Makkah with the intention of performing Umrah and encouraged all Muslims to participate in this journey. A group refrained, but a large number of Muhajirin, Ansar, and desert-dwelling Arabs set out in his service toward Makkah.

This group, numbering about one thousand four hundred people, were all wearing the Ihram garments and carried no weapons of war except for the sword, which was considered the weapon of a traveler.

[^1]: This interpretation was chosen by Abu al-Futuh Razi, Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, the author of Fi Zilal in his commentary, and Fayd Kashani in Al-Safi. Meanwhile, in Tafsir al-Tibyan, Al-Kashshaf, Fakhr Razi, and some others, the second interpretation (Conquest of Makkah) is preferred. The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan listed both views along with others but mentioned the Conquest of Makkah as the first view, which indicates his preference for it.

When the Prophet reached "Usfan" near Makkah, he learned that the Quraysh had decided to prevent him from entering Makkah. Eventually, the Prophet ص reached "Hudaybiyyah" (Hudaybiyyah is a village twenty kilometers from Makkah, named after a well or a tree that was there). The Prophet said, "Stop right here." They said, "There is no water here." Through a miracle, the Prophet ص provided water for his companions from a well that was there.

At this point, envoys traveled back and forth between the Quraysh and the Prophet ص so that the problem might be resolved in some way. Finally, "Urwah ibn Mas'ud al-Thaqafi," who was a shrewd man, came to the Prophet ص on behalf of the Quraysh. The Prophet ص said, "I have not come for war; my only goal is to visit the House of Allah." During this meeting, Urwah observed the scene of the Prophet ص performing ablution (wudu), where the companions would not allow a single drop of his ablution water to fall to the ground. Upon returning to the Quraysh, he said: "I have visited the courts of Khosrow, Caesar, and the Negus; I have never seen a ruler among his people held in such greatness as Muhammad ص is among his companions. If you imagine they will abandon Muhammad ص, you are greatly mistaken. You are facing such self-sacrificing individuals; make your decision."

In the meantime, the Prophet ص suggested to "Umar" that he go to Makkah and inform the nobles of Quraysh of the purpose of this journey. Umar said, "The Quraysh have intense enmity toward me, and I fear them. It is better that 'Uthman' undertakes this task." Uthman went to Makkah, and it was not long before a rumor spread among the Muslims that he had been killed. At this point, the Prophet ص decided on firm action and renewed the pledge with his companions under a tree that was there, which became known as the "Pledge of Ridwan" (Bay'at al-Ridwan). He made a covenant with them to resist until the last breath. However, it was not long before Uthman returned safely, and following him, the Quraysh sent "Suhayl ibn Amr" to the Prophet ص for reconciliation, but they emphasized that his entry into Makkah was absolutely impossible that year.

After much discussion, a peace treaty was concluded. One of its articles was that the Muslims would forgo the Umrah that year and come to Makkah the following year, provided they did not stay for more than three days and brought no weapons other than those of a traveler. Numerous other articles were included regarding the safety of the lives and property of Muslims entering Makkah from Madinah, as well as a 10-year cessation of war between the Muslims and the polytheists, and the freedom of the Muslims of Makkah to perform their religious duties.

This treaty was, in fact, a comprehensive non-aggression pact that temporarily ended the continuous and repeated wars between the Muslims and the polytheists.

The "Text of the Peace Treaty" was as follows: The Prophet ص commanded Ali عليه السلام to write:

"In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful." Suhayl ibn Amr, the representative of the polytheists, said: "I am not familiar with such a phrase; write 'In Your name, O Allah' (Bismika Allahumma)." The Prophet ص said: "Write: Bismika Allahumma."

Then he said: "Write: This is what Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah ص, has reconciled with Suhayl ibn Amr." Suhayl said: "If we recognized you as the 'Messenger of Allah,' we would not have fought you. Write only your own name and your father's name." The Prophet ص said: "There is no objection; write: 'This is what Muhammad ibn Abdillah has settled with Suhayl ibn Amr: that there shall be a cessation of war for ten years so that the people may regain their security.'"

Furthermore, whoever comes from the Quraysh to Muhammad without the permission of their guardian (and becomes Muslim), they shall be returned; but whoever from those with Muhammad returns to the Quraysh, their return is not required.

Everyone is free: whoever wishes to enter the covenant of Muhammad may do so, and whoever wishes to enter the covenant of the Quraysh may do so.

Both parties are committed not to betray one another (and to respect each other's lives and property).

Beyond this, Muhammad shall return this year and not enter Makkah. However, next year, we will leave Makkah for three days, and his companions may come, but they shall not stay for more than three days (to perform the Umrah rituals and return), on the condition that they carry no weapons other than the traveler's weapon—the sword—and that in its scabbard.

A group of Muslims and polytheists witnessed this peace treaty, and the scribe of the treaty was Ali ibn Abi Talib. [^1] The late "Allamah Majlisi" in Bihar al-Anwar narrated some other points, including:

"Islam must be manifest in Makkah, no one shall be forced in their choice of religion, and no harm or harassment shall reach the Muslims." [^2] This meaning was also present in the previous expression in general.

At this point, the Prophet ص commanded that the sacrificial camels they had brought be sacrificed right there, that they shave their heads, and exit the state of Ihram.

However, this was very difficult for a group of Muslims, as exiting Ihram without performing the Umrah rituals seemed impossible to them. But the Prophet ص personally took the lead, slaughtered the sacrificial camels, and exited Ihram, making the Muslims understand that this was an exception in the law of Ihram and sacrifice established by Allah.

When the Muslims saw this, they submitted, and the Prophet’s ص command was precisely executed. From there, they set out for Madinah, but a mountain of grief and sorrow weighed heavily on their hearts, for the outward appearance of the entire journey seemed to be a failure and defeat. They were unaware of what victories for the Muslims and the future of Islam lay behind the story of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. It was at this moment that Surah al-Fath was revealed, giving the glad tidings of a great victory to the Noble Prophet of Islam. [^1]

The Political, Social, and Religious Consequences of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah

A brief comparison between the situation of the Muslims in the sixth year of the Hijrah (at the time of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah) and two years later—when they set out with ten thousand equipped soldiers for the Conquest of Makkah to give a crushing response to the polytheists' breach of the treaty and finally opened Makkah without the slightest military clash because the Quraysh saw no power of resistance in themselves—shows how extensive the impact of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah was.

In short, the Muslims gained several important advantages and victories from this peace, as follows:

  1. They practically showed the deceived people of Makkah that they did not intend slaughter and that they held great respect for the holy city of Makkah and the House of Allah. This very fact caused the hearts of a large number of people to be drawn toward Islam.

  2. The Quraysh, for the first time, officially recognized Islam and the Muslims, which was evidence of the stabilization of their position in the Arabian Peninsula.

  3. After the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, Muslims could easily travel everywhere, their lives and property were protected, and they practically came into close contact with the polytheists—a contact whose result was a greater understanding of Islam by the polytheists and the drawing of their attention to Islam.

  4. After the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, the way was opened for the spread of Islam throughout the Arabian Peninsula. The fame of the Prophet’s ص peace-seeking nature forced various tribes, who had a wrong perception of Islam and the person of the Prophet ص, to reconsider. Vast possibilities in terms of propagation fell into the hands of the Muslims.

  5. The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah paved the way for the conquest of "Khaybar" and the removal of this cancerous tumor of the Jews, which was a significant actual and potential danger to Islam and the Muslims.

  6. Fundamentally, the Quraysh's fear of engaging with the Prophet’s army of one thousand four hundred, who carried no significant weapons of war, and their acceptance of the peace terms was itself a major factor in strengthening the morale of the supporters of Islam and the defeat of the opponents, who took the Muslims into account to such an extent.

  7. After the event of Hudaybiyyah, the Prophet ص wrote numerous letters to the leaders of the great countries of Iran, Rome, and Abyssinia, and the great kings of the world, inviting them to Islam. This clearly shows to what extent the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah had given self-confidence to the Muslims, such that they were making their way not only in the Arabian Peninsula but in the great world of that time.

Now we return to the commentary of the verses.

From what has been said, it can be clearly understood that the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah was truly a great opening and victory for Islam and the Muslims, and it is no surprise that the Holy Quran refers to it as a "Clear Victory" (Fathun Mubina).

Furthermore, there are several other clues that support this interpretation:

  1. The phrase "فتحنا" (We have granted victory) is in the past tense, showing that this matter had been realized at the time of the revelation of the verses, while nothing but the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah was in effect.

  2. The time of the revelation of these verses, mentioned above, and other verses of this Surah that praise the believers and condemn the hypocrites and polytheists in the event of Hudaybiyyah, are further evidence of this meaning.

Verse 27 of this Surah, which emphasizes the Prophet’s ص "truthful vision," says: "You shall certainly enter the Masjid al-Haram, if Allah wills, in security... and perform the rituals of Umrah." This is a clear witness that this Surah and its content were after Hudaybiyyah and before the Conquest of Makkah.

  1. In numerous narrations, the "Treaty of Hudaybiyyah" is introduced as the "Clear Victory." For example:

In the commentary Jawami' al-Jami', it is stated: When the Prophet was returning from Hudaybiyyah (and Surah al-Fath was revealed), one of the companions said: "What victory is this, when we were barred from the House and our sacrificial animals were blocked?" The Prophet ص said:

"بئس الكلام هذا، بل هو اعظم الفتوح، قد رضى المشركون ان يدفعوكم عن بلادهم بالراع، و يسئلوكم القضية، و رغبوا اليكم فى الامان و قد رأوا منكم ما كرهوا!:"

"Evil is what you have said! Rather, it is the greatest of victories, for the polytheists were satisfied to turn you away from their land without a violent clash, and they asked you for a settlement, and they desired peace from you after they had seen from you what they disliked!" [^1] Then the Prophet reminded them of the hardships they endured at Badr and al-Ahzab. The Muslims then acknowledged that this was the greatest of victories and that they had judged out of ignorance. [^2] "Zuhri," who is among the famous figures of the Tabi'un, says: "No victory greater than the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah took place, for the polytheists came into contact with the Muslims and Islam took root in their hearts, and within three years, a vast group embraced Islam, and the population of Muslims increased through them." [^3] In these Hadiths, some of the advantages that reached the Muslims by the blessing of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah are mentioned.

Only in one Hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام is it mentioned that:

"إِنَّا فَتَحْنا" was revealed after the "Conquest of Makkah." [^4] However, since the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah was a prelude to the Conquest of Makkah two years later, reconciling this Hadith will not be difficult.

In other words, the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah in the short term caused the Conquest of Khaybar (in the seventh year of the Hijrah) and beyond that, it caused the Conquest of Makkah, as well as the victory of Islam on the entire world stage in terms of influencing people's hearts.

In this way, one can combine the four interpretations, with the caveat that the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah forms the primary axis.

[^1]: Jawami' al-Jami' (according to Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 48, Hadith 9). [^2]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 68. [^3]: Ibid, p. 109. [^4]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 48.

Ayahs

لِّيَغْفِرَ لَكَ ٱللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِن ذَنۢبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكَ وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (٢)
that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come, and that He may perfect His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path, (2)
وَيَنصُرَكَ ٱللَّهُ نَصْرًا عَزِيزًا (٣)
and Allah will help you with a mighty help. (3)

Commentary

The Great Results of the Manifest Victory

In these two verses, some of the blessed outcomes of the "Manifest Victory" (the Peace of Hudaybiyyah) mentioned in the previous verse are explained. He says: "That Allah may forgive for you what preceded of your sin and what will follow and complete His favor upon you and guide you to a straight path" (لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ ما تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِكَ وَ ما تَأَخَّرَ وَ يُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ وَ يَهْدِيَكَ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً).

"And [that] Allah may aid you with a mighty victory" (وَ يَنْصُرَكَ اللَّهُ نَصْراً عَزِيزاً). Thus, Allah bestowed four great blessings upon His Prophet in the shadow of this manifest victory: forgiveness, the completion of favor, guidance, and victory.

Key Points

1- Answering Several Important Questions

Many questions have been raised here, and commentators from the earliest times until now have addressed them. Specifically, the following three questions concern the first divine blessing, namely the blessing of "forgiveness of past and future sins":

1- Since the Prophet, by virtue of his status of Infallibility ('Ismah), is pure from every sin, what is the meaning of this sentence?

2- Even if we set aside this objection, what is the connection between the "Victory of Hudaybiyyah" and the "forgiveness of sins"?

3- If the phrase "what will follow" (ma ta'akhkhara) refers to future sins, how is it possible for a sin that has not yet occurred to be forgiven? Is this not a license to commit sins in the future?

Each commentator has attempted to resolve these difficulties in some way. However, to arrive at the most comprehensive answer and a precise interpretation of these verses, a preliminary explanation seems necessary:

The key is to find the relationship between the "Victory of Hudaybiyyah" and the issue of "forgiveness of sin," which holds the primary key to answering the three aforementioned questions.

By carefully examining historical events, we reach this conclusion: when a true school of thought appears and stands tall, those loyal to superstitious traditions—who see their existence threatened—attribute every kind of slander and false accusation to it. They create rumors, fabricate lies, and count various "sins" against its leader, waiting to see what his ultimate end will be.

If this school of thought faces defeat in its path of progress, it provides a strong pretext for opponents to prove their false attributions, and they shout: "Did we not say it was so? Did we not say it was such?"

But when it achieves victory and its programs emerge successfully from the crucible of trial, all false attributions vanish into thin air. All the "did we not say so's" turn into regret and remorse, replaced by "we did not know!"

Specifically regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, these false attributions and "imaginary sins" were very numerous. They considered him a warmonger, an instigator of strife, indifferent to true traditions, impossible to negotiate with, and the like.

The Peace of Hudaybiyyah clearly showed that his religion, contrary to what the enemies imagined, was a progressive and divine faith. Its Quranic verses guaranteed the cultivation of human souls and the ending of oppression, tyranny, war, and bloodshed.

He respects the House of Allah; he never attacks a people or group without reason; he is a man of logic and reason; his followers love him; he truly invites all humans toward their Beloved, Allah; and if his enemies do not impose war upon him, he seeks peace and tranquility.

In this way, the Victory of Hudaybiyyah washed away all the "sins" that they might have attributed to him before the Hijrah, after the Hijrah, or all the sins before this event and even those they might attribute in the future. Since Allah granted this victory to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it can be said that Allah washed them all away.

The conclusion is that these sins were not real sins, but rather imaginary sins in the minds and beliefs of the people. Just as we read in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 14, regarding the story of Musa عليه السلام, where Musa says to Allah: "And they have a sin against me, so I fear they will kill me" (وَ لَهُمْ عَلَيَّ ذَنْبٌ فَأَخافُ أَنْ يَقْتُلُونِ). While his "sin" was nothing but helping an oppressed person from the Children of Israel and striking an oppressor from the Pharaoh's people.

Clearly, this was not only not a sin but was an act of supporting the oppressed; however, from the perspective of the Pharaoh's people, it was considered a sin.

In other words, "dhanb" (sin) linguistically means the "evil consequences" and "after-effects" of an action. The emergence of Islam initially disrupted the lives of the polytheists, but subsequent victories caused those consequences to be forgotten.

Whenever an old, dilapidated house that is our current shelter and to which we are attached is demolished, we might criticize this act. But after a strong and well-equipped building is constructed in its place and all discomforts are removed, our judgment changes completely.

The polytheists of Mecca, both before and after the Hijrah, had incorrect perceptions of Islam and the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which subsequent victories invalidated.

Yes, if we consider the relationship between the forgiveness of these sins and the Victory of Hudaybiyyah, the matter becomes perfectly clear—a relationship derived from the "Lam" (for) in "That Allah may forgive you" (Liyaghfira laka Allah), which is the code to unlocking the meaning of the verse.

However, those who did not pay attention to this point have questioned the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله status of Infallibility and attributed (God forbid) sins to him that Allah supposedly forgave in light of the Victory of Hudaybiyyah, or they interpreted the verse contrary to its apparent meaning.

Among their claims: some said it refers to the sins of the Ummah! Others said it refers to the sins people committed against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, such as harassment, which ceased with the Victory of Hudaybiyyah (in this case, "sin" is attributed to the object, not the subject)!

Or they took it to mean "leaving the better option" (tark al-awla). Or they interpreted it as hypothetical sins: that if, hypothetically, you were to commit a sin in the future or had in the past, We would forgive them.

But it is clear that all of these are forced interpretations without evidence. If we compromise the Infallibility of the Prophets, their very purpose of existence vanishes, for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله must be a role model in everything. How can a sinful person fulfill this role? Furthermore, he himself would then need another leader and guide to direct him.

Other interpretations are also highly contrary to the apparent meaning, and the major flaw is that they sever the connection between the forgiveness of sin and the Peace of Hudaybiyyah.

The best interpretation is the one mentioned above, which answers all three questions at once and clarifies the connection between the phrases of the verse.

All of this concerns the first of the four blessings Allah gave His Prophet in the light of the "Peace of Hudaybiyyah." As for the "completion of the Lord's favor," "guidance to the smooth and straight path," and "invincible divine victory" after the victory of Hudaybiyyah, these are not hidden from anyone. Islam spread rapidly, conquered ready hearts, the greatness of its teachings became clear to all, it neutralized poisonous propaganda, completed the divine favor, and paved a straight path toward great victories, such that in the event of the Conquest of Mecca, the army of Islam opened the enemy's most important stronghold without any resistance.

2- What is meant by "what preceded" and "what will follow"?

In the verse under discussion, we read that Allah says: in the shadow of this manifest victory, He has forgiven your preceding and following sins. There is discussion among commentators as to what "preceding" (ma taqaddama) and "following" (ma ta'akhkhara) refer to.

Some have considered "what preceded" to refer to the disobedience and tark al-awla of Adam and Eve, and "what will follow" to refer to the sins of the Ummah. Others consider "what preceded" to be matters related to before the Prophethood and "what will follow" to be after the Prophethood. Others still relate "what preceded" to what happened before the "Peace of Hudaybiyyah" and "what will follow" to what occurred after it.

However, given the interpretation we provided regarding the core meaning of the verse—and especially the relationship of this forgiveness with the Victory of Hudaybiyyah—it becomes clear that it refers to all the false attributions and "sins" that they, in their own view, attributed to the Noble Prophet in the "past" and "future." Had this great victory not been achieved, they would have considered all these "sins" as certain. But with this victory, both the past false attributions were dismantled, and those they might have attributed in the future were prevented.

Another witness to this interpretation is a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Ridha عليه السلام. When Ma'mun asked about this verse, the Imam replied:

"No one's sin was heavier in the eyes of the polytheists of Mecca than that of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, for they worshipped 330 idols. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله invited them to Monotheism (Tawhid), it was very hard for them, and they said: 'Has he made the gods [into] one God? This is a curious thing... We have not heard of this in the religion of our forefathers; this is nothing but a fabrication.'

But when Allah opened Mecca for His Prophet (after the Peace of Hudaybiyyah), Allah said: 'O Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, We have provided a manifest victory for you so that He may forgive the past and future sins you had and would have in the eyes of the Arab polytheists because of your call to Monotheism.' This is because some polytheists of Mecca had believed by that day, and some left Mecca without believing but were unable to deny Monotheism; thus, the Prophet's 'sin' in their eyes was also forgiven due to the victory." When Ma'mun heard this, he said: "Bravo, O Abu al-Hasan!" (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 56).

Ayah

هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ ٱلسَّكِينَةَ فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدَادُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنًا مَّعَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (٤)
It is He who sent down composure into the hearts of the faithful that they might enhance in their faith. To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (4)

Commentary

The Descent of Sakinah (Tranquility) upon the Hearts of the Believers

What we read in the previous verses were the great blessings that Allah bestowed upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in the light of the "Manifest Victory" (the Peace of Hudaybiyyah). However, in the verse under discussion, it speaks of a magnificent blessing He granted to all the believers, stating: "It is He who sent down tranquility into the hearts of the believers that they would increase in faith along with their [present] faith" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ).

Why should tranquility and peace not descend upon their hearts "while to Allah belong the soldiers of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is Knowing and Wise" (وَ لِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً).

What was this Sakinah?

It is necessary here to return to the story of the "Peace of Hudaybiyyah" and imagine ourselves in the atmosphere of Hudaybiyyah and the environment that emerged after the peace treaty, in order to become acquainted with the depth of the verse's meaning.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) had seen a dream—a divine and merciful vision—that he and his companions were entering Masjid al-Haram. Following this, he set out with the intention of visiting the House of Allah. Most of the companions thought the interpretation of this dream and righteous vision would occur during this very journey, whereas something else had been decreed. This was one aspect.

On the other hand, the Muslims had entered the state of Ihram and brought sacrificial animals with them, but contrary to their expectations, they were not granted the success of visiting the House of Allah. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) ordered them to slaughter the sacrificial camels right there in Hudaybiyyah and exit the state of Ihram. This was extremely difficult and unbelievable for them, as their customs and traditions, as well as Islamic rulings, required that they not exit Ihram until they had performed the rites of Umrah.

Thirdly, the terms of the Hudaybiyyah peace treaty contained points that were very heavy to accept. For instance, if someone from the Quraysh became Muslim and sought refuge in Medina, the Muslims had to return him to his family, but the reverse was not required! Fourthly, during the drafting of the peace treaty, the Quraysh were not willing for the words "Messenger of Allah" to appear next to the name of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله), and Suhayl, the representative of the Quraysh, insisted on its removal. He did not even agree to the writing of "بسم اللَّه الرحمن الرحيم" (In the name of Allah, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful) and insisted that "باسمك اللهم" (In Your name, O Allah) be written instead, which was consistent with the tradition of the people of Mecca.

It is clear that each of these matters alone was a source of distress, let alone all of them combined. For this reason, a tremor fell into the hearts of those with weak faith. Even when Surah al-Fath was revealed, some asked in astonishment: "What victory?!" It is here that Divine Grace must encompass the Muslims and return tranquility and peace to their hearts, so that not only would no weakness or lethargy find its way into them, but rather, according to the phrase "لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ", the strength of their faith would be increased. The above verse was revealed under such conditions.

"Sakinah" is originally derived from the root sukun, meaning the peace and reassurance of heart that eliminates any kind of doubt, hesitation, or terror from a person and keeps them steadfast in the storm of events.

This peace may have an ideological aspect, removing instability of belief, or a practical aspect, in a way that grants a person steadfastness, resistance, and patience. Of course, in accordance with the preceding discussions and the expressions of the verse itself, it refers more to the first meaning here, whereas in verse 248 of Surah al-Baqarah, in the story of "Talut" (Saul) and "Jalut" (Goliath), it emphasizes the practical aspects more.

A group of commentators have mentioned other meanings for "Sakinah", which ultimately return to this same interpretation.

Interestingly, in some narrations, "Sakinah" has been interpreted as "faith" [1], and in others as a heavenly breeze that appears in human form and grants peace to the believers [2]. These also support what has been said, because "Sakinah" is born of faith and is as soothing as a heavenly breeze.

It is also noteworthy that regarding "Sakinah", the term "Inzal" (sending down) is used. As we know, this expression in the Holy Quran is sometimes used in the sense of creation, origination, and the granting of a blessing; and because it is from a high station to a lower one, this expression is used.

[1] and [2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 114.

Key Points

1. Unparalleled Peace!

If faith had no fruit other than this matter of peace, it would be enough for a person to welcome it with their entire being, let alone its other fruits and blessings.

An examination of the state of believers and faithless individuals clarifies the reality that the second group lives in a state of constant anxiety and worry, while the first group enjoys unparalleled reassurance of heart, and in its shadow:

They never fear anyone but Allah: "وَ لا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللَّهَ" (Al-Ahzab: 39).

The blame and reproach of this person or 그 person never affects their iron will: "وَ لا يَخافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لائِمٍ" (Al-Ma'idah: 54).

They never grieve over what they have lost, nor do they have an intense attachment to what they possess. These two principles ensure that their spiritual peace is not shaken by the past or the future: "لِكَيْلا تَأْسَوْا عَلى‌ ما فاتَكُمْ وَ لا تَفْرَحُوا بِما آتاكُمْ" (Al-Hadid: 23).

And finally, they never become weak in the face of harsh events, nor do they allow sorrow to enter them, and they always see themselves as superior to the enemy: "وَ لا تَهِنُوا وَ لا تَحْزَنُوا وَ أَنْتُمُ الْأَعْلَوْنَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ" (Al-Imran: 139).

The believer does not see himself as alone in the field of events; he constantly feels the hand of Allah's grace and support over his head and senses the help of the angels within his being.

In contrast, the anxiety prevailing over faithless individuals is completely perceptible through their speech and behavior, especially when the storms of events blow.

2. The Hierarchy of Faith

Whether faith is defined as knowledge, awareness, and recognition, or as a spirit of submission and acceptance before the Truth, it possesses degrees and a hierarchy. This is because knowledge has degrees, and acceptance and submission also have various levels; even the passion, fervor, and love coupled with faith vary.

The verse under discussion, which says: "لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ" (that they would increase in faith along with their faith), is also an emphasis on this reality. For this reason, a believer should never stop at one stage of faith; he constantly steps toward higher degrees through self-purification, knowledge, and action.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "Faith has ten degrees, like a ladder from which one climbs rung after rung!" [1].

And in another hadith from him, it is stated: "Allah has divided faith into seven portions: goodness, truthfulness, certainty, contentment, loyalty, knowledge, and forbearance. Then He distributed it among the people. Whoever has all these seven portions is a complete and committed believer, and for some people, He placed one portion, two portions, and for some three portions, up to seven portions."

Then the Imam (عليه السلام) added: "Do not carry upon the owner of one portion what is the duty of the owner of two portions, and do not place upon the owner of two portions what pertains to the owner of three portions, lest their burden becomes heavy and they fall into hardship" [2]! From this, it becomes clear that what some have quoted—that faith does not increase or decrease—is very baseless, as it aligns neither with scientific realities nor with Islamic narrations.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 69, p. 165. [2]: Al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter: Degrees of Faith (Hadith 1).

3. Two Important Means of Peace

At the end of the verse under discussion, we read two phrases, each of which expresses one of the factors of the believers' "Sakinah" and peace: First, the phrase "وَ لِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ" (To Allah belong the soldiers of the heavens and the earth and they are under His command). Then the phrase "وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً" (And Allah is Knowing and Wise).

The first tells man: "If you are with Allah, all the forces of the earth and heaven are with you," and the second tells him: "Allah knows your needs, problems, and difficulties, and He is aware of your efforts, struggles, obedience, and servitude."

With faith in these two principles, how is it possible for peace of mind not to prevail over a person's being?

Ayahs

لِّيُدْخِلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا وَيُكَفِّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا (٥)
That He may admit the faithful, men and women, into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and that He may absolve them of their misdeeds. That is a great success with Allah. (5)
وَيُعَذِّبَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ ٱلظَّآنِّينَ بِٱللَّهِ ظَنَّ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۚ عَلَيْهِمْ دَآئِرَةُ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۖ وَغَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَنَهُمْ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا (٦)
That He may punish the hypocrites, men and women, and the polytheists, men and women, who entertain a bad opinion of Allah. For them shall be an adverse turn of fortune: Allah is wrathful with them and He has cursed them, and prepared hell for them and it is an evil destination. (6)
وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (٧)
To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (7)

Commentary

Another Result of the "Manifest Victory"

A group of Shiite and Sunni commentators have narrated that when the glad tidings of "Manifest Victory," the "completion of favor," "guidance," and "succor" were given to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in the opening verses of this Surah, some Muslims—who were distressed and anxious due to the events of "Hudaybiyyah"—remarked: "Congratulations to you, O Messenger of Allah! Allah has made clear to you what He will do with you, but what will He do with us?" Thereupon, the first verse under discussion was revealed, giving glad tidings to the believers that great rewards had also been prepared for them [^1]: "That He may admit the believing men and the believing women..."

In any case, these verses continue to speak in connection with the Peace of Hudaybiyyah, its various reflections in people's minds, and its fruitful results, defining the destiny of each group in this great crucible of trial.

First, He says: "Another objective of this great victory was that He may admit the believing men and the believing women into gardens beneath which rivers flow" (لِيُدْخِلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

"To abide therein forever," and this great blessing shall never be taken away from them (خالِدِينَ فِيها).

Furthermore, the goal was that He "remit from them their evil deeds" and grant them His forgiveness (وَ يُكَفِّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ).

"And that is, in the sight of Allah, a great success"! (وَ كانَ ذلِكَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, vol. 26, p. 85; Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, vol. 10, p. 26; Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, vol. 26, p. 86.

In this manner, in exchange for the four favors Allah bestowed upon His Prophet in the "Manifest Victory," He also granted two magnificent favors to the believers: eternal Paradise with all its blessings, and the pardon and forgiveness of their lapses—in addition to the Sakinah (tranquility) and spiritual peace He granted them in this world. The sum of these three blessings constitutes a "Great Success" and a grand victory for those who emerged safely from this crucible of trial.

The word Fawz (success/victory), which is usually mentioned in the Holy Quran with the description ‘Azim (Great), and sometimes accompanied by Mubin (Manifest) or Kabir (Big), means—according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat—achieving victory and attaining goodness coupled with safety. This is realized when it includes salvation in the Hereafter, even if it is accompanied by the loss of material worldly benefits.

According to well-known narrations, when the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, had his blessed forehead struck in the mihrab of worship by the sword of the criminal of the age, Abd al-Rahman ibn Muljam, he cried out: Fuztu wa Rabbil-Ka'bah: "By the Lord of the Ka'bah, I have succeeded!" (And the record of my felicity was signed with the blood of my head!).

Indeed, sometimes the trials of the Lord are so severe and grueling that they uproot weak faiths and overturn hearts; only the true believers, who benefit from the blessing of Sakinah and tranquility, remain steadfast and will also benefit from its consequences in the Resurrection—and this is truly a great success.

However, in contrast to this group, there was a group of hypocrites and faithless polytheists whose fate is depicted in the following verse: "Another objective is that He may punish the hypocritical men and hypocritical women, and the polytheistic men and polytheistic women" (وَ يُعَذِّبَ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَ الْمُنافِقاتِ وَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ وَ الْمُشْرِكاتِ). "Those who harbor an evil thought about Allah" (الظَّانِّينَ بِاللَّهِ ظَنَّ السَّوْءِ).

Yes, the hypocrites, at the time of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers' departure from Medina, thought that this group would never return safely to Medina, as we read in verse 12 of this same Surah: "Nay, but you thought that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families."

The polytheists also thought that Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, with this small group and lack of sufficient weaponry, would not return safely to Medina, and that the star of Islam would soon set.

Then, He proceeds to explain this torment and punishment, describing it under four headings: He says: "Upon them shall be the evil turn of fortune" (عَلَيْهِمْ دائِرَةُ السَّوْءِ). [^1] Dairah literally means events and occurrences that happen to a person, whether good or bad, but here, by mentioning the word Su’ (evil), it refers to undesirable events.

Secondly, "Allah is wroth with them" (وَ غَضِبَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ).

[^1]: Su’ (with the vowel u) according to Sihah al-Lughah has an infinitive meaning, and Su’ (with the vowel o) has a gerundial meaning, but according to Al-Kashshaf, both have the same meaning.

And also, "He has cursed them"—meaning He has removed them from His mercy (وَ لَعَنَهُمْ).

And finally, "He has prepared Hell for them—and an evil destination it is" (وَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَهَنَّمَ وَ ساءَتْ مَصِيراً). [^1] It is noteworthy that in the scene of Hudaybiyyah, it was mostly Muslim men present, and on the opposite side, hypocritical and polytheistic men; yet in the above verses, the Quran considers women and men as partners in that "Great Success" and this "Painful Torment." This is because the believing men who appear on the battlefield would not reach their goals without the support of believing women, nor would the hypocritical men without the cooperation of hypocritical women.

Fundamentally, Islam is not a religion of men that ignores the personality of women; therefore, in every case where the omission of women's names might imply an exclusive meaning to the speech, it mentions them explicitly to make it clear that Islam belongs to all human beings.

In the final verse under discussion, He refers once again to the greatness of Allah's power, saying: "And to Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth; and Allah is All-Mighty, All-Wise" (وَ لِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً). This statement appeared once following the ranks and favors of the people of faith, and once here following the punishment of the hypocrites and polytheists, to clarify that the God under Whose command all the hosts of the heavens and the earth lie has the power for the former and the ability for the latter. Whenever the sea of His mercy surges, it encompasses the worthy wherever they may be, and whenever the fire of His wrath blazes, no criminal has the power to escape it.

It is noteworthy that when mentioning the believers, Allah is described with "Knowledge and Wisdom" (in some contexts) which suits the station of mercy, but regarding the hypocrites and polytheists, He is described with "Might and Wisdom" which suits the station of punishment.

What is meant by "the hosts of the heavens and the earth"? This term has a broad meaning that includes the armies of divine angels as well as armies such as lightning, earthquakes, storms, floods, waves, and other invisible forces of which we are unaware, for all are divine hosts and are subservient to His command.

Key Points Who Harbors Evil Thoughts About Allah?

"Evil suspicion" (Su' al-Zann) is sometimes toward oneself, sometimes toward others, and sometimes toward God, just as "good suspicion" (Husn al-Zann) also has a three-fold division.

However, evil suspicion toward oneself—provided it does not reach an extreme—is a ladder for perfection; it causes a person to be strict and meticulous regarding their own actions and prevents vanity and pride arising from good deeds. For this reason, Ali عليه السلام says in the famous "Hammam" sermon describing the pious: "They are suspicious of themselves and fearful of their deeds. When one of them is praised, he becomes frightened of what is said about him and says: 'I know myself better than others, and my Lord knows me better than I know myself! O Allah! Do not take me to task for what they say, and make me better than what they think, and forgive me for what they do not know!'"

But if this evil suspicion is regarding people, it is forbidden, except in times when corruption prevails over society, where being overly trusting is not correct. (The explanation of this will come, God willing, under verse 12 of Surah al-Hujurat).

As for "evil suspicion" toward Allah—meaning toward His promises, His infinite mercy, and His bounty—it is very ugly and repulsive, and a sign of weak faith or sometimes a lack of faith. The Quran repeatedly mentions the evil suspicions of faithless or weak-faithed individuals, especially during the occurrence of severe social events and storms of trial; how the believers remain steadfast in these moments with complete good suspicion and trust in the Lord's grace, while the weak and incapable open their mouths in complaint. Just as in the story of the Victory of Hudaybiyyah, the hypocrites and their like-minded associates harbored evil thoughts and said: "Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and his companions are going on this journey and will not return," as if they had forgotten the divine promises or were pessimistic toward them.

A clear example of this appeared especially on the battlefield of the Trench (Ahzab) when the Muslims were under severe pressure, and Allah severely rebuked the evil suspicions of a group: "When they came upon you from above you and from below you, and when eyes grew wild and hearts reached the throats, and you harbored [evil] thoughts about Allah. There the believers were tried and shaken with a mighty shaking" (Al-Ahzab, verses 10-11).

Even in verse 154 of Al-Imran, He called such thoughts "the thought of ignorance" (Zann al-Jahiliyyah). In any case, the matter of having good suspicion toward Allah and His promise of mercy, bounty, grace, and favor is one of the important signs of faith and one of the effective means of salvation and felicity.

To the extent that it is narrated in a hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: "There is no servant who thinks well of Allah except that Allah treats him according to his thought!" [^1].

And in another hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام: "Think well of Allah, for Allah, the Mighty and Sublime, says: 'I am near the thought of My believing servant toward Me; if it is good, then good [will be his treatment], and if it is evil, then evil!'" [^2].

And finally, in another hadith from the Prophet, it is said: "Good suspicion toward Allah, the Mighty and Sublime, is the price of Paradise" [^3]. What price is easier than this? And what commodity is more valuable than that?

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, vol. 70, p. 384. [^2] and [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, vol. 70, p. 385.

Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ شَـٰهِدًا وَمُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا (٨)
Indeed We have sent you as a witness, and as a bearer of good news and warner, (8)
لِّتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَتُعَزِّرُوهُ وَتُوَقِّرُوهُ وَتُسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا (٩)
that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and that you may support him and revere him, and that you may glorify Him morning and evening. (9)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَايِعُونَكَ إِنَّمَا يُبَايِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ يَدُ ٱللَّهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۚ فَمَن نَّكَثَ فَإِنَّمَا يَنكُثُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِمَا عَـٰهَدَ عَلَيْهُ ٱللَّهَ فَسَيُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (١٠)
Indeed those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah: the hand of Allah is above their hands. Then whosoever breaks his oath, breaks it only to his own detriment, and whoever fulfills the covenant he has made with Allah, He will give him a great reward. (10)

Commentary

Strengthening the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) Position and the People's Duties Toward Him

As we have mentioned, the Peace of Hudaybiyyah was severely criticized by some uninformed individuals, who even used expressions toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that were not devoid of disrespect. The sum of these events necessitated that the position and greatness of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) be emphasized once again.

Therefore, in the first verse under discussion, addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it says:

"Indeed, We have sent you as a witness, a bringer of glad tidings, and a warner" (إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ شاهِداً وَ مُبَشِّراً وَ نَذِيراً).

These three great descriptions and three prominent statuses are among the most important roles of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): being a "witness" (shahid), a "bringer of glad tidings" (bashir), and a "warner" (nadhir). He is a witness over the entire Muslim Ummah, and in a sense, a witness over all nations, as stated in Surah An-Nisa, verse 41: "So how [will it be] when We bring from every nation a witness and we bring you, [O Muhammad], against these [people] as a witness?" (فَكَيْفَ إِذا جِئْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ وَ جِئْنا بِكَ عَلى‌ هؤُلاءِ شَهِيداً).

And in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 105, He says: "And say, 'Work, for Allah will see your deeds, and [so will] His Messenger and the believers'" (وَ قُلِ اعْمَلُوا فَسَيَرَى اللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَ رَسُولُهُ وَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ). Here, "the believers" refers to the Infallible Imams (علیهم السلام).

In principle, every human being has many witnesses.

Before anyone else, Allah, who is the Knower of the Unseen and the Witnessed, observes all our actions and intentions.

After Him, the "angels" are commissioned to record human deeds, as alluded to in Surah Qaf, verse 21: "And every soul will come, with it a driver and a witness" (وَ جاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَعَها سائِقٌ وَ شَهِيدٌ).

Then, the "limbs of the human body" and even the skin will testify:

"On a Day when their tongues, their hands and their feet will bear witness against them as to what they used to do" (Surah An-Nur, 24) (يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

"And they will say to their skins, 'Why have you testified against us?' They will say, 'We were made to speak by Allah, who has made everything speak; and He created you the first time, and to Him you are returned'" (Surah Fussilat, 21) (وَ قالُوا لِجُلُودِهِمْ لِمَ شَهِدْتُمْ عَلَيْنا قالُوا أَنْطَقَنَا اللَّهُ الَّذِي أَنْطَقَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

The "earth" is also among the witnesses, as mentioned in Surah Az-Zalzalah: "That Day, it will report its news" (يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبارَها).

According to some narrations, "time" will also be in the rank of witnesses on that Day. In a Hadith from Ali (عليه السلام), we read:

"No day passes for the son of Adam except that it says to him: 'I am a new day, and I am a witness over you. So do good in me and perform righteous deeds in me, so that I may testify in your favor on the Day of Resurrection; for indeed, you shall never see me again after this!'" 1

Undoubtedly, Allah's testimony alone is sufficient, but the multiplicity of witnesses serves both to further establish the proof (itmam al-hujjah) and to have a stronger educational and moral effect on human beings.

In any case, the Holy Quran in this verse presents three important missions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)—the matter of witnessing, glad tidings, and warning—as three major attributes, to serve as a prelude to the duties mentioned in the following verse.

[1]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 112.

In the next verse, five important commands are stated as the result and objective of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) aforementioned attributes. Two commands concern obedience to Allah and His glorification and worship, and three commands concern "obedience," "defense," and "veneration" of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) status. It says: "[The goal is] that you [people] may believe in Allah and His Messenger and honor him and respect him and exalt Him [i.e., Allah] morning and afternoon" (لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ وَ تُعَزِّرُوهُ وَ تُوَقِّرُوهُ وَ تُسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَ أَصِيلًا).

"Tu'azziruhu" (تُعَزِّرُوهُ) is derived from the root "ta'zir," which originally means "to prevent" or "to hinder." Subsequently, it has been applied to any kind of defense, support, and assistance against enemies. Certain punishments that prevent sin are also called "ta'zir."

"Tuwaqqiruhu" (تُوَقِّرُوهُ) is from the root "tawqir," derived from "waqr," meaning weight or gravity. Therefore, "tawqir" here means veneration, honoring, and showing great respect.

According to this interpretation, the pronouns in "tu'azziruhu" and "tuwaqqiruhu" refer to the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and the goal is to defend him against enemies and to venerate and honor him. (This interpretation was chosen by Shaykh Tusi in "Al-Tibyan," Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan," and others).

However, a group of commentators [1] believe that all the pronouns in the verse refer to Allah, and the meaning of "ta'zir" and "tawqir" here is to aid the religion of Allah and to venerate Him and His faith. Their reason for choosing this interpretation is the consistency of all the pronouns in the verse.

But the first interpretation seems more appropriate because, firstly: the primary meaning of "ta'zir" is prevention and defense against an enemy, which is not correct regarding Allah except in a metaphorical sense. More importantly, the occasion of revelation (shan al-nuzul) of the verse was after the event of Hudaybiyyah, when some had shown disrespect toward the lofty status of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and the verse was revealed to guide the Muslims regarding their duties toward the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Furthermore, it should not be forgotten that this verse serves as a conclusion to the previous verse, which describes the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) as a "witness," "bringer of glad tidings," and "warner," and this provides the context for the commands that follow in the next verse.

[1]: Zamakhshari in "Al-Kashshaf," Alusi in "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Fayd Kashani in "Tafsir al-Safi," and Allameh Tabataba'i in "Al-Mizan" have accepted this interpretation.

In the final verse under discussion, a brief reference is made to the "Pledge of Ridwan" (Bay'at al-Ridwan), which is described in more detail in verse 18 of this same Surah.

To explain: As we have said, according to famous historical accounts, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), following a dream he had seen, left Medina with 1,400 people with the intention of performing Umrah. However, near Mecca, the polytheists decided to prevent him and his companions from entering the city. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his companions stopped at the land of "Hudaybiyyah," and envoys were exchanged between him and the Quraysh, which eventually led to the Peace Treaty of Hudaybiyyah.

During these missions, Uthman was once commissioned by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to deliver the message to the people of Mecca that he had not come for war, but only intended to visit the House of Allah. However, the polytheists temporarily detained Uthman, and this caused a rumor of his murder to spread among the Muslims. If such a thing were true, it would have been a declaration of war by the Quraysh. Therefore, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said, "We shall not leave this place until we fight this people," and to emphasize this important matter, he invited the people to renew their pledge of allegiance to him. The Muslims gathered under a tree that was there and pledged to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that they would never turn their backs on the battlefield and would strive to the best of their ability to uproot the enemy.

This news reached the polytheists of Mecca and struck fear and terror into their hearts, which caused them to submit to this (for them) unpleasant peace.

This pledge is called the "Pledge of Ridwan" because verse 18 of this Surah states: "Certainly Allah was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree" (لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ).

In any case, the Holy Quran says in the verse under discussion: "Indeed, those who pledge allegiance to you, [O Muhammad]—they are actually pledging allegiance to Allah. The hand of Allah is over their hands" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُبايِعُونَكَ إِنَّما يُبايِعُونَ اللَّهَ يَدُ اللَّهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ).

"Bay'at" (pledge of allegiance) means making a covenant for obedience and submission to someone. It was customary that the one making the covenant of obedience would place his hand in the hand of his leader and thereby express his oath of loyalty.

Since hands were also joined during "buying and selling" (bay') to conclude a transaction, the word "bay'at" was applied to these covenants, especially since in their covenant, they were, as it were, putting their lives up for transaction with the person to whom they declared loyalty.

From this, the meaning of "The hand of Allah is over their hands" (يَدُ اللَّهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ) becomes clear. This expression is a metaphor indicating that the pledge with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is a divine pledge; it is as if the hand of Allah is placed above their hands. They are not just pledging to His Prophet, but to Allah Himself. Such metaphors are very common in the Arabic language.

Therefore, those who have interpreted this sentence as "the power of Allah is above their power" or "the help and victory of Allah is superior to the help of the people" and the like, do not align well with the occasion of revelation and the context of the verse, even though the statement itself is true in essence.

Then it adds: "So he who breaks his word only breaks it to the detriment of himself" (فَمَنْ نَكَثَ فَإِنَّما يَنْكُثُ عَلى‌ نَفْسِهِ).

"And he who fulfills that which he has promised Allah—He will give him a great reward" (وَ مَنْ أَوْفى‌ بِما عاهَدَ عَلَيْهُ اللَّهَ فَسَيُؤْتِيهِ أَجْراً عَظِيماً). [1]

"Nakth" (نکث) is from the root "nakth" (rhyming with makth), meaning to untwist or unravel. Subsequently, it has been used for breaking a covenant or violating an oath. 2 In this verse, the Holy Quran warns all those who pledged allegiance that if they remain faithful to their covenant and oath, they will have a great reward, but if they break it, the harm will return to themselves. They should not imagine they are harming Allah; rather, they are endangering the survival of society, their own greatness, honor, strength, power, and even their very existence by breaking the covenant.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), it is narrated:

"Indeed, in Hell there is a city called 'Al-Hasinah.' Will you not ask me what is in it?" It was said to him: "What is in it, O Commander of the Faithful?" He said: "In it are the hands of the covenant-breakers!"

From this, it becomes clear how ugly and repulsive the issue of breaking a covenant and violating a pledge is from the perspective of Islam. [1] Regarding the original subject of "Bay'at in Islam" and even "before Islam," the "quality of the pledge," and "its rulings," there are discussions that, God willing, will be presented under verse 18 of this same Surah.

[1]: It should be noted that "alayhu" (عَلَيْهُ) in the above verse, contrary to the usual practice, is read with a dammah on the "ha." Some commentators have justified this by saying that this is the same "ha" of "huwa" which is originally dammah-voweled. After the omission of the "waw," it sometimes remains dammah-voweled, like "lahu" and "anhu," and sometimes, because it is preceded by a "ya," it becomes kasra-voweled, like "alayhi." However, in the verse under discussion, because it is followed by the word "Allah," it is read with a dammah to be more compatible with the "tafkhim" (heavy pronunciation) of the "lam" in "Allah." 2: "Nakth" with a fatha on the "nun" has a gerundial meaning, and "nikth" with a kasra on the "nun" has the meaning of a noun of the gerund. [1]: "Bihar al-Anwar," Vol. 67, p. 186.

Ayahs

سَيَقُولُ لَكَ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ شَغَلَتْنَآ أَمْوَٰلُنَا وَأَهْلُونَا فَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا ۚ يَقُولُونَ بِأَلْسِنَتِهِم مَّا لَيْسَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا إِنْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ ضَرًّا أَوْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ نَفْعًۢا ۚ بَلْ كَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًۢا (١١)
The Bedouins who had stayed back [from joining the Prophet in his ‘umrah journey to Makkah] will tell you, ‘Our possessions and families kept us occupied. So plead [to Allah] for our forgiveness!’ They will say with their tongues what is not in their hearts. Say, ‘Whether He desires to cause you harm or desires to bring you benefit, who can be of any avail to you against Allah[’s will]? Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do.’ (11)
بَلْ ظَنَنتُمْ أَن لَّن يَنقَلِبَ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِيهِمْ أَبَدًا وَزُيِّنَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ وَظَنَنتُمْ ظَنَّ ٱلسَّوْءِ وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًۢا بُورًا (١٢)
Rather, you thought that the Apostle and the faithful will not ever return to their folks, and that was made to seem decorous to your hearts; you entertained evil thoughts, and you were a ruined lot. (12)
وَمَن لَّمْ يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ فَإِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ سَعِيرًا (١٣)
Those who have no faith in Allah and His Apostle [should know that] We have prepared a blaze for the faithless. (13)
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٤)
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth: He forgives whomever He wishes, and punishes whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (14)

Commentary

The Excuses of the Defaulters!

In the preceding verses, we mentioned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله set out from Medina toward Mecca with one thousand four hundred Muslims with the intention of performing Umrah.

An announcement was made by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله among the Bedouin tribes that they should all accompany him on this journey. However, a group of those weak in faith refused to obey this command. Their analysis was: how could it be possible for the Muslims to survive this journey unscathed, while the Quraysh disbelievers had previously been in an aggressive state and had imposed the battles of Uhud and Ahzab upon the Muslims right at the edge of Medina?

Now that this small, unarmed group was walking into Mecca by their own feet and placing themselves next to a hornet's nest, how could they possibly return to their homes?! However, when they saw that the Muslims returned safely to Medina with full hands and significant advantages gained from the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah—without a single drop of blood being shed—they realized their great mistake. They came to the Prophet to apologize in some way, justify their actions, and request the Prophet to seek forgiveness for them.

But the above verses were revealed, pulling back the curtain on their conduct and exposing them.

Thus, after mentioning the fate of the hypocrites and polytheists in the previous verses, the Quran here recounts the situation of the weak-faithed defaulters to complete the links of this discussion.

It says: "The defaulters among the Bedouins will soon say to you [as an excuse]: 'Our possessions and our families kept us occupied, so we could not be in your service on this blessed journey! Now, accept our excuse and ask forgiveness for us!'" (سَيَقُولُ لَكَ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ شَغَلَتْنا أَمْوالُنا وَ أَهْلُونا فَاسْتَغْفِرْ لَنا).

"They say with their tongues what is not in their hearts" (يَقُولُونَ بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ ما لَيْسَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ‌).

They are not even sincere in their repentance.

But tell them: "Who then can prevail for you at all against Allah if He intends you any harm, or if He intends you any benefit?" (قُلْ فَمَنْ يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئاً إِنْ أَرادَ بِكُمْ ضَرًّا أَوْ أَرادَ بِكُمْ نَفْعاً).

It is not difficult at all for Allah to afflict you with various calamities and misfortunes in your secure homes and alongside your wives, children, and wealth. Likewise, it is not difficult for Him to keep you safe from any harm even in the center of your enemies and the heart of the opposition. It is your ignorance of Allah's power that makes such thoughts seem fair-seeming to you.

Indeed, "Allah is All-Aware of all that you do" (بَلْ كانَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً).

Rather, He is well aware of the secrets within your chests and your intentions. He knows well that these excuses and pretexts have no reality; what is real is your doubt, hesitation, fear, and weakness of faith. These fabrications are not hidden from Allah and will never prevent your punishment.

It is interesting that both the tone of the verses and historical accounts suggest that these verses were revealed during the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله return to Medina. That is, before the defaulters could come and make their excuses, the Quran pulled back the curtain and disgraced them!

Then, to further explain, it pulls the curtain completely aside and adds: "Nay, but you thought that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families" (بَلْ ظَنَنْتُمْ أَنْ لَنْ يَنْقَلِبَ الرَّسُولُ وَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِلى‌ أَهْلِيهِمْ أَبَداً).

Yes, the reason for your non-participation in this historic journey was not the issue of wealth, wives, or children; rather, the primary factor was the ill-suspicion you had toward Allah. With your wrong calculations, you thought that this journey was the final journey of the life of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers, and that you should stay away from it.

Indeed, "this false notion and these satanic whispers were made fair-seeming in your hearts" (وَ زُيِّنَ ذلِكَ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ‌).

"And you conceived an evil thought" (وَ ظَنَنْتُمْ ظَنَّ السَّوْءِ).

For you thought that Allah had sent His Prophet on this journey only to hand them over to the claws of the enemies and would not support them! "And you became a ruined people!" (وَ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً بُوراً).

What ruin is worse than being deprived of participating in this historic journey, the Pledge of Ridwan, and other honors, followed by great disgrace and the painful punishment of the Hereafter in the future? Indeed, you had dead hearts to have met such a fate.

Since these weak-faithed or hypocritical individuals are cowardly and comfort-seeking people, naturally fleeing from war and any kind of conflict, the analysis they provide regarding events never aligns with reality. Nevertheless, it always appears very attractive to them.

In this way, fear, comfort-seeking, and fleeing from responsibility make their ill-suspicions appear as realities in their eyes, and they become pessimistic about everything—even toward the Prophet of God and even toward Allah! In Nahj al-Balagha, in the command to Malik al-Ashtar, we read:

"Miserliness, cowardice, and greed are diverse instincts gathered together by ill-suspicion toward Allah." [1]

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 53.

The story of Hudaybiyyah and the verses under discussion are the objective manifestation of this very meaning, showing how ill-suspicion toward the Lord stems from ugly traits such as miserliness, greed, and cowardice.

Since these wrong stances sometimes stemmed from a lack of faith, the next verse says: "And whoever does not believe in Allah and His Messenger, then surely We have prepared for the disbelievers a blazing Fire" (وَ مَنْ لَمْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ فَإِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ سَعِيراً). [1] "Sa'ir" means "blazing."

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, to prove Allah's power to punish the disbelievers and hypocrites, it says: "To Allah belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth. He forgives whom He wills and punishes whom He wills, and Allah is Ever-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (وَ لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ يَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً).

It is noteworthy that here, the issue of forgiveness is mentioned before the issue of punishment, and at the end of the verse, the emphasis is again on divine forgiveness and mercy. This is because the goal of all these threats and warnings is education and cultivation (tarbiyah), and the matter of education requires that the path of return remains open to sinners and even disbelievers—especially since the source of many of these negative stances is ignorance. For such individuals, the hope for forgiveness must be increased so that they might find the right path.

[1]: The phrasing of the verse would normally require saying "We have prepared for them a blazing Fire," but the pronoun was intentionally replaced with the explicit noun "the disbelievers" (al-kafirin) to state the cause of this wretched fate, which is the issue of disbelief.

Key Points Justifying Sin is a Universal Malady!

No matter how heavy a sin may be, it is not as heavy as the justification of that sin. This is because a sinner who admits to their sin often seeks repentance, but the tragedy begins when justifications come into play. These not only close the path of repentance to the person but also make them more entrenched and audacious in their sin.

This justification is sometimes for the sake of saving face and preventing disgrace before people, but it is even worse when it is done to deceive one's own conscience.

This act of justification is nothing new; various examples of it can be found throughout human history, showing how the great criminals of history resorted to ridiculous justifications to deceive themselves or others, leaving any observer in total amazement.

The Holy Quran, which is a great lesson in education and human-building, has many discussions on this subject, an example of which we read in the verses above.

It is worthwhile to examine other examples to complete this discussion:

  1. The Arab polytheists, to justify their polytheism, sometimes appealed to the customs of their ancestors, saying: "Indeed, we found our fathers upon a religion, and we are following in their footsteps" (Az-Zukhruf: 23). And sometimes they appealed to a kind of fatalism, saying: "If Allah had willed, neither we nor our fathers would have associated partners [with Him]" (Al-An'am: 148).

  2. Sometimes weak-faithed believers, to flee from war, would come to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and leave the scene under the pretext that their houses did not have proper walls and were vulnerable: "And a group of them asked permission of the Prophet, saying: 'Indeed, our houses are exposed,' while they were not exposed. They intended nothing but to flee" (Al-Ahzab: 13).

  3. Sometimes, under the pretext that if they went to war with the Romans, the beautiful Roman women might steal their hearts and they would fall into sin, they would ask the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for permission not to participate: "And among them is he who says: 'Permit me [to stay at home] and do not put me into trial'" (At-Tawbah: 49).

  4. Sometimes, under the title that their wealth, wives, and children had preoccupied them, they justified the great sin of fleeing from obedience to the Prophet's command (as in the verses under discussion).

  5. Satan also justified his explicit disobedience to Allah with a false comparison, saying: "You created me from fire and created him from clay! How can a more noble being prostrate to a lower being?": "I am better than him; You created me from fire and created him from clay" (Al-A'raf: 12).

  6. In the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), to justify the great crime of "infanticide," they would say: "We fear that in wars our daughters might fall into the hands of enemies; our sense of honor requires us to bury female infants under the earth!" And sometimes they said: "If our children remain alive, we are unable to provide for their livelihood" (Al-Isra: 31).

It even appears from some Quranic verses that sinners will resort to certain things to justify their sins on the Day of Resurrection, including saying: "We followed the leaders of our people, and it was they who led us astray and made decisions for us!": "Our Lord, indeed we obeyed our masters and our grandees, and they led us astray from the [right] path" (Al-Ahzab: 67).

In short, the plague of "justification" is a pervasive one that encompasses a vast group of people, both the common and the elite. Its great danger is that it closes the paths of reform to sinners and sometimes makes realities appear distorted even in the person's own eyes.

There are many who justify their "fear and cowardice" as "caution," their "greed" as "securing the future," their "rashness" as "decisiveness," their "weakness of soul" as "modesty," their "incompetence" as "asceticism," their "committing the forbidden" as a "legal loophole," their "fleeing from responsibility" as the "subject not being proven," and their "weaknesses and shortcomings" as "divine decree and destiny." How painful it is for a person to close the door of salvation upon themselves with their own hands!
