Although each of these concepts has a correct meaning in its proper place, the problem lies in distorting them and drawing the opposite conclusion. What immense damage has reached human societies, families, and individuals through this path! May Allah protect us all from this great and home-destroying plague (Amen).

Ayahs

سَيَقُولُ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا ٱنطَلَقْتُمْ إِلَىٰ مَغَانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوهَا ذَرُونَا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ ۖ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُبَدِّلُوا۟ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُل لَّن تَتَّبِعُونَا كَذَٰلِكُمْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ فَسَيَقُولُونَ بَلْ تَحْسُدُونَنَا ۚ بَلْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٥)
[In the near future] when you will set out to capture booty, those who stayed behind [in this journey] will say, : ‘Let us follow you.’ They desire to change the word of Allah. Say, ‘You will never follow us! Allah has said thus beforehand.’ Then they will say, ‘You are envious of us.’ Indeed, they do not understand but a little. (15)
قُل لِّلْمُخَلَّفِينَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ سَتُدْعَوْنَ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ أُو۟لِى بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ تُقَـٰتِلُونَهُمْ أَوْ يُسْلِمُونَ ۖ فَإِن تُطِيعُوا۟ يُؤْتِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَجْرًا حَسَنًا ۖ وَإِن تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ كَمَا تَوَلَّيْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٦)
Say to the Bedouins who stayed behind, ‘[Later on] you will be called against a people of a great might: they will either embrace Islam, or you will fight them. So if you obey, Allah will give you a good reward; but if you turn away like you turned away before, He will punish you with a painful punishment.’ (16)
لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ ۗ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ يُعَذِّبْهُ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٧)
There is no blame on the blind, nor is there any blame on the lame, nor is there blame on the sick [if they are unable to go out with the troops to face the enemies]; and whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, He will admit him into gardens with streams running in them, and whoever refuses to comply, He will punish him with a painful punishment. (17)

Commentary

Ready-to-Claim Defaulters!

Most commentators believe that these verses refer to the "Conquest of Khaybar," which took place after the "Peace of Hudaybiyyah" at the beginning of the seventh year of the Hijrah.

To explain: according to narrations, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was returning from Hudaybiyyah, by Allah's command, He gave the Muslims who participated in Hudaybiyyah the glad tidings of the "Conquest of Khaybar." He explicitly stated that only they should participate in this battle, and the spoils of war would belong exclusively to them, while those who had defaulted (stayed behind) would have no share in these spoils. However, as soon as these cowardly world-seekers perceived through various signs that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would certainly be victorious in this upcoming war and that abundant spoils would fall to the army of Islam, they seized the opportunity. They came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked for permission to participate in the field of Khaybar! Perhaps they even resorted to the excuse that: "To compensate for our past mistake, to lighten our burden of responsibility, to repent for our sin, and to serve Islam and the Quran sincerely, we wish to join you in this field of Jihad!" They were unaware that the verses of the Quran had already been revealed, exposing their secret, as we read in the first verse under discussion:

"When you set out to take spoils, those who remained behind will soon say, 'Let us follow you,' [wishing to participate in this Jihad]!" (سَيَقُولُ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا انْطَلَقْتُمْ إِلى‌ مَغانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوها ذَرُونا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ‌).

Not only in this instance, but in other cases as well, we see these greedy, comfort-seeking individuals pursuing "fat morsels" that involve little trouble, while fleeing from difficult, dangerous, and distant battlefields. As we read in verse 42 of Surah At-Tawbah: "Had it been a near gain and an easy journey, they would have followed you, but the distance (to the field of Tabuk) was too far for them. Yet they will soon swear by Allah, 'If we could have, we would have gone forth with you.'" (لَوْ كانَ عَرَضاً قَرِيباً وَ سَفَراً قاصِداً لَاتَّبَعُوكَ وَ لكِنْ بَعُدَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الشُّقَّةُ وَ سَيَحْلِفُونَ بِاللَّهِ لَوِ اسْتَطَعْنا لَخَرَجْنا مَعَكُمْ‌).

In any case, the Quran, in the verses under discussion, responds to this profit-seeking and opportunistic group by saying: "They wish to change the Word of Allah" (يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُبَدِّلُوا كَلامَ اللَّهِ‌).

Then it adds: "Say: You shall never follow us" and you have no right to participate in this field (قُلْ لَنْ تَتَّبِعُونا).

This is not something I say of my own accord; "This is what Allah has said before" and He has informed us of your future (كَذلِكُمْ قالَ اللَّهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

Allah has commanded that the "spoils of Khaybar" be exclusive to the "People of Hudaybiyyah," and no one else shall share it with them! Yet these shameless and pretentious defaulters still do not back down and accuse you of jealousy: "And they will soon say, 'Nay, but you are jealous of us!'" (فَسَيَقُولُونَ بَلْ تَحْسُدُونَنا).

In this way, they even implicitly deny the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and consider the root of their exclusion from the "Ghazwa of Khaybar" to be jealousy! The Quran states in the final phrase: "Nay, but they understand but little" (بَلْ كانُوا لا يَفْقَهُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا).

Yes, the root of all their misfortunes is ignorance and lack of awareness that has always plagued them—ignorance regarding Allah, lack of recognition of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله status, unawareness of the destiny of mankind, and disregard for the transience of worldly wealth.

It is true that they were clever and meticulous in financial matters and personal interests, but what ignorance is greater than a human exchanging everything for a small amount of wealth?!

Ultimately, according to historical accounts, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله divided the spoils of Khaybar only among the People of Hudaybiyyah. He even allocated a share for those who were at Hudaybiyyah but were unable to participate in the Ghazwa of Khaybar; of course, there was only one such instance, and that was "Jabir bin Abdullah." [1]

[1]: Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 3, p. 364.

Continuing this discussion with the defaulters of Hudaybiyyah, the next verse makes a proposal to them, opening a path for their return as follows:

"Say to the defaulters among the Bedouins: You will soon be called to [fight] a people of great might; you shall fight them, or they shall submit [to Islam]" (قُلْ لِلْمُخَلَّفِينَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ سَتُدْعَوْنَ إِلى‌ قَوْمٍ أُولِي بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ تُقاتِلُونَهُمْ أَوْ يُسْلِمُونَ‌).

"Then if you obey, Allah will give you a good reward; but if you turn away as you turned away before, He will punish you with a painful punishment" (فَإِنْ تُطِيعُوا يُؤْتِكُمُ اللَّهُ أَجْراً حَسَناً وَ إِنْ تَتَوَلَّوْا كَما تَوَلَّيْتُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً).

If you are truly remorseful for your previous behavior and have abandoned comfort-seeking and worldliness, you must prove your sincerity in another difficult and formidable field. Otherwise, avoiding hard battlefields while trying to participate in easy and spoil-rich ones is not possible at all; it is evidence of your hypocrisy or weakness of faith, cowardice, and fear.

It is interesting that the Quran repeatedly emphasizes the title Mukhallafin (defaulters) in these verses, using the "explicit noun" instead of a "pronoun."

This expression specifically appears in the form of the "passive participle" (Ism al-Maf'ul), meaning "those who were left behind," indicating that when the faithful Muslims observed the lethargy and excuse-making of this group, they left them behind and rushed toward the field of Jihad, indifferent to their condition.

However, as to who this "people of great might" mentioned in this verse were, there is discussion among commentators.

The phrase "you shall fight them, or they shall submit" (تُقاتِلُونَهُمْ أَوْ يُسْلِمُونَ‌) is evidence that they were not People of the Book (Ahl al-Kitab), because the People of the Book are not forced to accept Islam; rather, they are given the choice between embracing Islam or accepting the conditions of Dhimmi status, peaceful coexistence with Muslims, and paying the Jizyah. It is only the polytheists and idolaters from whom nothing but Islam is accepted, as Islam does not recognize idolatry as a "religion," and compulsion in abandoning idolatry is permissible.

Given that during the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله era, after the events of Hudaybiyyah and Khaybar, there was no major Ghazwa against polytheists except the Conquest of Mecca and the Ghazwa of Hunayn, the above verse could refer to them—especially the Ghazwa of Hunayn, in which the fierce warriors of the Hawazin and Bani Sa'd tribes participated.

As for the possibility suggested by some that it refers to the Ghazwa of Mu'tah against the Romans, it seems unlikely because they were People of the Book.

The possibility that it refers to the wars after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, including the wars with Persia and Yamamah, is even more unlikely, as the tone of the verses indicates the matter pertains to the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and there is no necessity to apply it to wars after his era. Apparently, certain political motives influenced the thinking of some commentators who insisted on this point! It is also noteworthy that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله does not promise them that they will gain spoils in future wars, because the goal of Jihad is not to acquire spoils; rather, He emphasizes that Allah will give them a "good reward," a term usually used for rewards in the Hereafter.

Question

A question arises here: In verse 83 of Surah At-Tawbah, He completely rejects these outsiders, saying: "Say: You shall never go out with me, ever, nor shall you fight an enemy with me. Indeed, you were content with sitting [at home] the first time, so sit with those who stay behind." (فَقُلْ لَنْ تَخْرُجُوا مَعِيَ أَبَداً وَ لَنْ تُقاتِلُوا مَعِيَ عَدُوًّا إِنَّكُمْ رَضِيتُمْ بِالْقُعُودِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ فَاقْعُدُوا مَعَ الْخالِفِينَ‌).

How is it that the verse under discussion invites them to fight in another difficult and formidable field?

The answer becomes clear when we consider that the verse in Surah At-Tawbah concerns the defaulters of the Battle of Tabuk, of whom the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had lost hope, while the verse under discussion speaks of the defaulters of Hudaybiyyah, of whom hope had not yet been lost.

Since there were individuals among the defaulters who truly lacked the ability to participate in Jihad due to physical disability or illness, and their rights should not be overlooked, the final verse under discussion specifies their excuse. Especially since some commentators have narrated that after the revelation of the previous verse and the threat of "painful punishment" for the defaulters, a group of disabled or sick individuals came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked: "O Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله! What is our duty in this matter?"

At this point, this verse was revealed, stating their ruling as follows: "There is no blame on the blind, nor on the lame, nor on the sick [if they do not participate in the field of Jihad]" (لَيْسَ عَلَى الْأَعْمى‌ حَرَجٌ وَ لا عَلَى الْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَ لا عَلَى الْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ‌).

It is not only Jihad that is conditional upon ability and power; all divine obligations have a set of general conditions, among which is "ability and power," and this meaning is repeatedly mentioned in the verses of the Quran. In verse 286 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we read as a general principle: "Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity" (لا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها).

This condition is proven both by traditional evidence and by reason.

However, although this group is exempt from participating in the field of Jihad, they too must strive to the best of their ability to strengthen the forces of Islam and advance its divine goals, as we read in verse 91 of Surah At-Tawbah: "There is no blame on the weak, nor on the sick, nor on those who find nothing to spend [for Jihad], provided they are sincere to Allah and His Messenger" (لَيْسَ عَلَى الضُّعَفاءِ وَ لا عَلَى الْمَرْضى‌ وَ لا عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ ما يُنْفِقُونَ حَرَجٌ إِذا نَصَحُوا لِلَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ‌).

This means that if they are unable to perform a task with their hands, they should not withhold what they can do with their tongues. This is a beautiful expression showing that everyone must not neglect what is within their power; in other words, if they cannot participate at the front, they should at least keep the "home front" strong.

Perhaps the final sentence of the verse under discussion also refers to this meaning, stating:

"And whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger, He will admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow; but whoever turns away, He will punish him with a painful punishment" (وَ مَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ وَ مَنْ يَتَوَلَّ يُعَذِّبْهُ عَذاباً أَلِيماً).

There is also the possibility that when an exception is made to a rule, some individuals might think of exploiting it, trying to place themselves among the excused. The Quran warns them that if they are not truly excused, they will face a painful punishment.

It is noteworthy that the issue of the blind, the lame, and the sick being excused is specific to "Jihad" (offensive war). However, in the matter of "Defense," everyone must defend the essence of Islam, the Islamic homeland, and life to the extent of their ability, and there are no exceptions in this regard.

Ayahs

۞ لَّقَدْ رَضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبَايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ فَعَلِمَ مَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنزَلَ ٱلسَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَثَـٰبَهُمْ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا (١٨)
Allah was certainly pleased with the faithful when they swore allegiance to you under the tree. He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down composure on them, and requited them with a victory near at hand (18)
وَمَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً يَأْخُذُونَهَا ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (١٩)
and abundant spoils that they will capture, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (19)

Commentary

Allah’s Pleasure with the Participants of the Bay‘at al-Ridwan

We have mentioned that during the events of Hudaybiyyah, envoys were exchanged between "the Prophet" صلى الله عليه وآله and "the Quraysh." Among them, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent "Uthman ibn Affan" (who was a relative of Abu Sufyan, a connection that apparently influenced his selection) as a representative to the polytheists of Mecca and the nobles of Quraysh to inform them of the truth: that the Muslims had not come for war, but rather their goal was to visit the House of Allah and honor the Kaaba. However, the Quraysh temporarily detained Uthman, and subsequently, a rumor spread among the Muslims that Uthman had been killed. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله declared, "I shall not leave this place until I have fought this group."

He then came under a tree that was there and renewed the pledge (Bay‘ah) with the people, asking them not to fall short in the struggle against the polytheists and that no one should turn his back on the field of Jihad. [^1] The news of this pledge spread through Mecca, striking great terror into the hearts of the Quraysh, leading them to release Uthman.

As we know, this pledge became famous as the "Bay‘at al-Ridwan" (The Pledge of God’s Pleasure); it caused the polytheists to tremble and served as a turning point in Islamic history.

The verses under discussion speak about this event.

First, He says: "Certainly Allah was well-pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree" (لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ).

The purpose of this "Bay‘ah" was to further unify the forces, boost morale, renew combat readiness, gauge opinions, and test the level of sacrifice of loyal friends.

This pledge breathed a new spirit into the body of the Muslims, for they were placing their hands in the hand of the Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and expressing their loyalty from the depths of their hearts.

Allah granted these self-sacrificing and selfless believers, who pledged allegiance to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله at this critical moment, four great rewards. The most important of all was this very spiritual reward—namely, His pleasure and satisfaction—just as we read in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 72: وَ رِضْوانٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ: "and Allah's pleasure is the greatest [of all blessings]."

Then He adds: "He knew what was in their hearts [of sincerity, faith, and readiness for loyalty to this covenant], so He sent down tranquility upon them" (فَعَلِمَ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ).

[^1]: Majma‘ al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

Such a tranquility that, while surrounded by a multitude of enemies in a place far from their own city and homes, amidst the ready weapons of the foe, and without having sufficient weapons themselves (since they had come for pilgrimage, not for war), they allowed no fear or terror into their hearts and stood firm and unshakable like a mountain.

And this was the second divine blessing upon them.

In principle, special divine favors and assistance encompass those who possess sincerity of intention and purity of heart.

Thus, we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Indeed, the poor believing servant says: 'O Lord! Provide for me so that I may do such and such of good deeds and paths of charity.' When Allah, the Mighty and Sublime, knows this from him through the sincerity of his intention, Allah records for him a reward equal to what would be recorded for him had he performed it; indeed, Allah is All-Embracing, Generous." [^1]

At the end of this verse, He points to the third blessing, saying: "and He rewarded them with a near victory" (وَ أَثابَهُمْ فَتْحاً قَرِيباً).

Yes, this victory, which according to most commentators was the "Conquest of Khaybar" (though some have considered it the Conquest of Mecca), was the third divine reward for these selfless believers.

The expression "near" (quriban) is a confirmation that the "Conquest of Khaybar" is intended, as this victory was realized at the beginning of the seventh year of the Hijrah, only a few months after the event of Hudaybiyyah.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, p. 199.

The fourth blessing that was granted to the Muslims following the Bay‘at al-Ridwan was abundant material spoils, as the next verse states: "And another reward is the many spoils that they will take" (وَ مَغانِمَ كَثِيرَةً يَأْخُذُونَها).

One of these was the spoils of Khaybar, which fell into the hands of the Muslims in a short time. Given the immense wealth of the Jews of Khaybar, these spoils were of extraordinary importance.

However, there is no definitive reason to limit the spoils to those of Khaybar; it could encompass the spoils of other Islamic battles that occurred after the victory of Hudaybiyyah.

And since the Muslims must be completely confident in this divine promise, He adds at the end of the verse: "And Allah is Almighty, All-Wise" (وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً).

If He commanded you to make peace at Hudaybiyyah, it was based on wisdom—a wisdom that the passage of time unveiled. And if He promises you a near victory and many spoils, He has the power to bring His promises to fruition.

In this way, the faithful and selfless Muslims, under the shadow of the Bay‘at al-Ridwan and the declaration of loyalty to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in those critical hours, achieved victory in this world and the hereafter, while the oblivious hypocrites and the fearful of weak faith burned in the fire of regret.

We conclude this discussion with a statement from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام. When he speaks of the steadfastness of the early Muslims and their unparalleled Jihad against the enemy, and rebukes his weak-willed audience, he says:

"When Allah saw our sincerity, He sent down humiliation upon our enemy and sent down victory upon us, until Islam became established, settling firmly in its lands. By my life, if we had behaved as you do, no pillar of the religion would have stood, and no branch of the tree of faith would have turned green! By Allah, you shall surely milk blood [instead of milk] and you shall follow it with regret!" [^1]

Key Points The Pledge (Bay‘ah) and Its Characteristics

Bay‘ah is derived from the root bay‘ (selling/trading), originally meaning the shaking of hands upon concluding a transaction, and was subsequently applied to the shaking of hands for a covenant of obedience. It was such that whenever someone wanted to declare loyalty to another, recognize him, and obey his command, he would pledge allegiance (bay‘ah) to him. Perhaps the application of this word in this sense was because each of the two parties made a commitment similar to the commitment of two traders toward one another. The one pledging was prepared to stand by his obedience sometimes even at the cost of his life, and sometimes at the cost of his wealth and children, while the one receiving the pledge took upon himself his protection and defense.

Ibn Khaldun says in the introduction to his history: "When they pledged allegiance to the leader, they would place their hands in his hand as a confirmation, so it resembled the action of the seller and the buyer." [^1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 56. [^1]: Muqaddimah Ibn Khaldun, p. 174.

Evidence indicates that bay‘ah is not an innovation of the Muslims, but rather a tradition that was prevalent among the Arabs before Islam. For this reason, at the dawn of Islam, when the tribes of "Aws" and "Khazraj" came from Medina to Mecca during the Hajj and pledged allegiance to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله at Aqaba, their encounter with the issue of bay‘ah was an encounter with a familiar matter. After that, the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله also renewed the pledge with the Muslims on various occasions, one instance of which was this "Bay‘at al-Ridwan" at Hudaybiyyah. Even more extensive was the pledge taken after the Conquest of Mecca, the details of which will be explained, God willing, in the commentary of "Surah al-Mumtahanah."

As for the manner of bay‘ah in general, it was such that the one pledging would place his hand in the hand of the one being pledged to, and through the language of state or speech, declare obedience and loyalty. Sometimes, within the pledge, conditions and limits were set—for example, a pledge to the extent of wealth, to the limit of life, or to the limit of everything, even the loss of wife and children.

Sometimes the pledge was to the limit of not fleeing, and sometimes it was to the limit of death (incidentally, both these meanings have been recorded in histories regarding the Bay‘at al-Ridwan).

The Prophet of Islam also accepted the pledge of women, but not through shaking hands. Rather, as recorded in histories, he would order a large vessel of water to be brought; he would dip his hand into one side of the vessel, and the women pledging would dip their hands into the other side.

Sometimes, during the bay‘ah, the performance or abandonment of certain actions was stipulated, just as the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله stipulated in the pledge with women after the Conquest of Mecca that "they should not associate partners with Allah, nor engage in unchastity, nor steal, nor kill their children, and other matters" (Surah al-Mumtahanah, verse 12).

In any case, there are various discussions regarding the rulings of bay‘ah, which we briefly mention here, although the issues of this discussion in Islamic jurisprudence are also somewhat shrouded in ambiguity:

1- The "nature of bay‘ah" is a type of contract and covenant between the one pledging on one side and the one receiving the pledge on the other. Its content is obedience, following, support, and defense of the one pledged to, and it has various degrees according to the conditions mentioned therein. From the tone of the Quranic verses and Hadiths, it is understood that bay‘ah is a type of binding contract (aqd lazim) on the part of the one pledging, and acting according to it is obligatory. Therefore, it falls under the general law of "Fulfill the contracts" (أَوْفُوا بِالْعُقُودِ) (Al-Ma'idah: 1).

Consequently, the one pledging does not have the right to rescind, but the one receiving the pledge, if he deems it appropriate, can release the person from the pledge and rescind it. In this case, the one who pledged becomes free from his obligation and covenant. [^1]

2- Some consider bay‘ah to be similar to "elections" or a type of it, whereas the issue of elections is exactly the opposite. That is, its nature is a type of creating responsibility, duty, and a post or position for the one elected—or in other words, a type of deputation (tawkil) to perform a task—even though this election also entails duties for the elector (like all forms of power of attorney), whereas bay‘ah is not like this.

In other words: elections are the granting of a position, and as we said, it is similar to deputation, whereas bay‘ah is a "commitment to obedience."

Although these two may resemble each other in some of their effects, this resemblance never means the unity of their concept and nature. Therefore, in the case of bay‘ah, the one pledging is not able to rescind, whereas in the case of elections, in many instances, the electors have the right to rescind, such that they can collectively dismiss the elected person from his position (note carefully).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 22, p. 72

[^1]: In the events of Karbala, we read that Imam Hussain عليه السلام delivered a sermon on the night of Ashura, and while expressing appreciation for his companions, he released them from his pledge so they could go wherever they wished (but they remained loyal nonetheless), saying: "Go, for you are released from my obligation; there is no bond from me upon you." (Kamil Ibn Athir, Vol. 4, p. 57).

3- In the case of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Imams عليهم السلام who are appointed by Allah, there is no need for bay‘ah. That is, obedience to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Imam عليه السلام and those appointed by him is obligatory, whether for those who have pledged allegiance or those who have not.

In other words: the requirement of the position of Prophethood and Imamate is the obligation of obedience, just as the Quran says: "Obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those in authority among you" (أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَ أُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ) (An-Nisa: 59).

However, the question arises: if this is so, then why did the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله repeatedly take pledges from his companions or new Muslims, two examples of which are explicitly mentioned in the Quran (the Bay‘at al-Ridwan here and the pledge with the people of Mecca mentioned in Surah al-Mumtahanah)?

In response, we say: undoubtedly, these pledges were a type of emphasis on loyalty that took place at specific times, and were used especially to confront crises and difficult events, so that under their shadow, a new spirit would be breathed into the individuals, as we read about its wonderful effects in the Bay‘at al-Ridwan in the previous discussions.

However, in the pledges taken for the Caliphs, the bay‘ah was for the acceptance of the position of the Caliphate, even though in our view, the succession to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was not something to be realized through the bay‘ah of the people, but rather was realized only from Allah and by means of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself or the preceding Imam.

For this reason, the pledge that the Muslims made with Ali عليه السلام or Imam Hasan or Imam Hussain عليهم السلام also had the aspect of emphasizing loyalty and was similar to the pledges of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

4- Is bay‘ah currently acceptable as an Islamic principle? Or in other words, can bay‘ah be generalized—for example, can a certain group choose a capable individual who meets the Sharia requirements (as a military commander, head of an association, or head of government) and pledge allegiance to him? Do such pledges fall under the Sharia rulings of bay‘ah?

Since there is no "general" or "absolute" text from the Quran and Sunnah specifically regarding the generalization of bay‘ah, generalizing this matter seems difficult, although arguing based on the generality of the verse "Fulfill the contracts" is not very far-fetched.

Nevertheless, the ambiguity that exists in matters related to bay‘ah prevents us from definitively relying on "Fulfill the contracts," especially since in our jurisprudence, no instance for bay‘ah is seen other than for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Imam عليه السلام.

It is also necessary to note that the position of the deputy of the Jurist (Wali al-Faqih), in our view, is a position designated by the Infallible Imams عليهم السلام and has no need for bay‘ah. Of course, the people's following and obedience to the Wali al-Faqih gives him the possibility of using this position and what is termed "extension of hand" (bast al-yad), but this does not mean that his position is contingent upon the people's following. Furthermore, the issue of the people's following is not related to the issue of bay‘ah, but is rather acting upon the divine command regarding the Guardianship of the Jurist (note carefully).

5- In any case, bay‘ah relates to executive matters and has no connection to the rulings (laws). That is, pledging allegiance to a person never gives him the right of "legislation and lawmaking"; rather, laws must be taken from the Book and the Sunnah and then implemented, and no one disputes this.

6- It is understood from the narrations that the pledge with the Infallible Imam and leader must be for the sake of Allah; in other words, it is among the matters in which "intention of proximity" (qasd al-qurbah) is necessary.

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"There are three whom Allah, the Mighty and Sublime, will not speak to on the Day of Resurrection, nor will He look at them, nor will He purify them, and for them is a painful punishment: A man who pledges allegiance to an Imam only for the sake of the world—if he gives him what he wants, he fulfills his pledge, and if not, he withholds; and a man who sells a commodity to a man after the Asr prayer, swearing by Allah, the Mighty and Sublime, that he was offered such and such for it, so the buyer believes him and takes it, while he was not given what he said; and a man who has surplus water in the wilderness and withholds it from the wayfarer." [^1] (The mention of the Asr time is either because of the nobility of this time, or because many sellers sell their goods at the price they bought them at this time).

7- Breaking a pledge is among the major sins. In a Hadith from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام, we read:

"Three things are destructive: breaking a deal (pledge), abandoning the Sunnah, and separating from the community." [^2] Abandoning the Sunnah apparently refers to the laws brought by the Prophet of Islam, and separating from the community means turning one's back on it, not merely not participating in the congregational prayer.

8- Bay‘ah in the words of Ali عليه السلام: In the sermons of Nahj al-Balagha, the issue of bay‘ah is repeatedly emphasized, and the Imam عليه السلام frequently relies on the pledge that the people made with him.

Among these, in one instance he says: "O people, you have a right over me, and I have a right over you. As for your right over me, it is that I be sincere and well-wishing toward you,

[^1]: Al-Khisal, Chapter of the Three, Hadith 70. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 67, p. 185.

and that I spend your public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) for your own sake, and that I teach you so that you may be saved from ignorance, and discipline you so that you may become aware." Then he adds:

"As for my right over you, it is the fulfillment of the pledge, and sincerity in presence and absence, and responding when I call you, and obedience when I command you." [^1]

And in another place he says:

"Your pledge to me was not a sudden, unconsidered act" (so that you should harbor the slightest doubt in obeying me). [^2]

In the sermon he delivered before the Battle of the Camel and the movement from Medina toward Basra, he drew the people's attention to remaining steadfast in their pledge, saying:

"And the people pledged allegiance to me without being disliked or forced, but rather obediently and by choice." [^3]

And finally, against Mu'awiyah, who refused to pledge allegiance to the Imam عليه السلام and wanted to find fault in some way, he said:

"The people who pledged allegiance to Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman pledged allegiance to me on the same basis they pledged to them. Thus, he who was present has no choice [to rescind], and he who was absent has no right to reject!" [^1]

From some expressions in Nahj al-Balagha, it is clearly understood that bay‘ah is only once; there is no way to reconsider it, and there is no choice to rescind it. Anyone who turns away from it is called a fault-finder and a critic, and he who thinks about or doubts its acceptance or rejection is a hypocrite!

"Indeed, it is one pledge; there is no second look in it, and no choice is renewed in it. He who leaves it is a critic, and he who deliberates in it is a sycophant!" [^2]

From the sum of these expressions, it is understood that the Imam عليه السلام, in the face of those who did not believe in his Imamate as designated by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and were making excuses, argued based on the issue of bay‘ah which was accepted by them, so that they would not have the power to refuse obedience to the Imam عليه السلام. He reminded Mu'awiyah and his likes that just as they recognized legitimacy for the caliphate of the three previous caliphs, they must recognize it for the caliphate of the Imam عليه السلام and submit to it (in fact, his caliphate was more legitimate because his pledge was more extensive and was done with general consent and desire).

Therefore, arguing based on bay‘ah is in no way inconsistent with the issue of the Imam's appointment by Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the confirmatory nature of the pledge.

Thus, in this very Nahj al-Balagha, in one instance the Imam refers to the Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (Thaqalayn), which is among the explicit texts of Imamate, [^3] and in another place to the issue of the testament (wasiyyah) and inheritance. [^4] (Note carefully).

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 34. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 136. [^3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 1. [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 6. It should be noted that the reliance on the pledge of the previous caliphs was because Mu'awiyah had been appointed by them and claimed to support them; therefore, it does not contradict what is stated in the Shaqshaqiyyah sermon. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 7. [^3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 87. [^4]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 2.

In his other expressions, he referred to the necessity of loyalty to the pledge, its permanence, the impossibility of rescinding or reconsidering it, and the lack of need for repetition—all of which are accepted matters regarding bay‘ah.

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from them that if the pledge has an aspect of compulsion or coercion, or is done by catching the people off guard, it has no value. Rather, the valuable pledge is that which is done by choice, free will, thought, and consideration (again, note carefully).

Ayahs

وَعَدَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ مَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً تَأْخُذُونَهَا فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هَـٰذِهِۦ وَكَفَّ أَيْدِىَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَنكُمْ وَلِتَكُونَ ءَايَةً لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (٢٠)
Allah has promised you abundant spoils, which you will capture. He has expedited this one for you, and withheld men’s hands from you, so that it may be a sign for the faithful, and that He may guide you to a straight path. (20)
وَأُخْرَىٰ لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا قَدْ أَحَاطَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرًا (٢١)
And other [spoils as well] which you have not yet captured: Allah has comprehended them, and Allah has power over all things. (21)

Commentary

Once Again, the Blessings of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah!

These verses continue to recount the discussions related to the "Treaty of Hudaybiyyah" and the events following it, explaining the blessings and benefits that accrued to the Muslims through this path.

First, He says: "Allah has promised you many spoils that you will capture, but He has hastened this one for you" (وَعَدَكُمُ اللَّهُ مَغانِمَ كَثِيرَةً تَأْخُذُونَها فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هذِهِ‌).

The tone of the verse indicates that the "many spoils" here refers to all the spoils that Allah granted to the Muslims, whether in the short term or the long term; some commentators even believe that the spoils that will fall to the Muslims until the Day of Resurrection are included in this expression.

However, regarding the phrase "He has hastened this one for you," it is generally considered an allusion to the "spoils of Khaybar," which were obtained shortly after the victory of Hudaybiyyah.

Yet, some have suggested the possibility that "this" (هذه) refers to the "Victory of Hudaybiyyah" itself, which was the greatest spiritual spoil.

Then, pointing to another of Allah's favors toward the Muslims in this event, He adds: "and He withheld the hands of the people from you" (وَ كَفَّ أَيْدِيَ النَّاسِ عَنْكُمْ‌).

This was a great favor—that despite their small numbers and lack of sufficient weaponry, and being in a location far from home right under the enemy's nose, they were not attacked. Allah cast such dread and terror of them into the hearts of the enemies that they refrained from any action or assault.

A group of commentators considers this sentence an allusion to the event of Khaybar, where the tribes of "Bani Asad" and "Bani Ghatafan" had decided to attack Medina in the absence of the Muslims, to plunder the Muslims' property and take their women captive.

Others consider it an allusion to the decision of a group from these two tribes who intended to rise to the aid of the Jews of Khaybar, but Allah cast terror into their hearts, and they abandoned their decision.

However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate, because in a few verses later, we see this same expression used regarding the people of Mecca; it serves as an elaboration for what is mentioned in the verse under discussion and is consistent with the Quranic method of brevity followed by detail.

The important point is that according to famous narrations, the entire Surah al-Fath was revealed after the event of Hudaybiyyah, during the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله journey back from Mecca to Medina.

Then, continuing the same verse, He points to two other great blessings among the Divine gifts, saying: "The goal was that these events might be a sign (of the truth of your call) for the believers, and that He might guide you to a straight path" (وَ لِتَكُونَ آيَةً لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ يَهْدِيَكُمْ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً). Although some commentators have taken the pronoun in "li-takuna" (that it may be) to refer only to the "promised spoils," and others to the restraining of the enemies from attacking the Muslims, it is more fitting that this pronoun refers to all the events of "Hudaybiyyah" and the occurrences following it. This is because each was a sign among the signs of Allah, a proof of the truthfulness of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and a means for guiding the people to the straight path. Part of it had the aspect of prophecy and news of the unseen, and some were inconsistent with ordinary causes and conditions; as a whole, it was considered a clear miracle among the miracles of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

In the next verse, He gives further glad tidings to the Muslims, saying: "And [He promises] other [spoils] which you were not able to achieve, but Allah has encompassed them, and Allah is Powerful over all things" (وَ أُخْرى‌ لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْها قَدْ أَحاطَ اللَّهُ بِها وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيراً). There is discussion among commentators as to which spoils and which victories this promise refers to.

Some consider it an allusion to the conquest of "Mecca" and the spoils of "Hunayn," while others refer it to the conquests and spoils that fell to the Islamic Ummah after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (such as the conquest of Persia, Rome, and Egypt).

The possibility also exists that it refers to all of them 1. The expression "which you were not able to achieve" (لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْها) indicates that the Muslims previously never imagined such conquests and spoils, but by the blessing of Islam and Divine aid, this strength and capability were created for them.

Some have inferred from this sentence that there had previously been a discussion among the Muslims regarding these conquests, but they saw themselves as incapable of achieving them. Specifically, in a Hadith regarding the story of the Battle of the Trench (Ahzab), we read: on the day the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله gave the Muslims the glad tidings of the conquest of Persia, Rome, and Yemen, the hypocrites mocked it.

The phrase "Allah has encompassed them" (قَدْ أَحاطَ اللَّهُ بِها) refers to the encompassment of the Lord's power over these spoils or conquests, and some have considered it an allusion to His encompassing knowledge; however, the first meaning is more consistent with the other phrases of the verse, though combining both is also permissible.

Finally, the last phrase of the verse, "and Allah is Powerful over all things" (وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيراً), is in fact like an explanation of the cause for the preceding sentence, indicating that with Allah's power over everything, such conquests are not strange for the Muslims.

In any case, the verse is among the reports of the unseen and the prophecies of the Holy Quran regarding future events. These victories occurred within a short period and clarified the greatness of these verses.

Key Point: The Event of the Battle of Khaybar

When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returned from Hudaybiyyah, he stayed in Medina for the entire month of Dhu al-Hijjah and part of Muharram in the seventh year of the Hijra. Then, he set out with one thousand four hundred of his companions who had participated in Hudaybiyyah toward "Khaybar" (a place that was a center of anti-Islamic provocations, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was counting the days for a suitable opportunity to dismantle that center of corruption).

The tribe of "Ghatafan" initially decided to support the Jews of "Khaybar," but later they became afraid and refrained.

When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله reached near the fortresses of "Khaybar," he ordered his companions to halt, then raised his head to the sky and recited this supplication:

"O Allah! Lord of the heavens and what they overshadow, and Lord of the earths and what they carry... we ask You for the good of this town and the good of its people, and we seek refuge in You from its evil, the evil of its people, and the evil of what is in it."

Then he said: "In the name of Allah, move forward," and thus they reached the outskirts of "Khaybar" by night. In the morning, when the people of "Khaybar" became aware of the situation, they found themselves besieged by the soldiers of Islam. Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله conquered the fortresses one after another, until he reached the last fortresses, which were the strongest and most powerful of all, where the famous Jewish commander "Marhab" was stationed.

During these days, a state of severe headache that occasionally afflicted the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came upon him, such that he could not leave his tent for a day or two. At this time (according to famous Islamic histories), "Abu Bakr" took the banner and charged with the Muslims toward the Jewish army, but returned without achieving a result. Once again, "Umar" took the banner and the Muslims fought more intensely than the day before, but they also returned without achieving a result.

This news reached the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and he said:

"By Allah, tomorrow I shall certainly give it to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger, and whom Allah and His Messenger love; he shall take it by force."

Necks were craned from every side to see who was intended. A group guessed that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله meant Ali عليه السلام, but Ali عليه السلام was not yet present there, as a severe eye ailment had prevented him from being with the army. However, in the morning, Ali عليه السلام arrived riding a camel and dismounted near the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله tent, while his eyes were in severe pain.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Come closer!" He went closer, and the Prophet rubbed some of his blessed saliva on Ali's عليه السلام eye, and his eye became completely healthy by the blessing of this miracle. Then he gave the banner to him.

Ali عليه السلام moved with the army of Islam toward the great fortress of Khaybar. A man from the Jews asked from atop the wall, "Who are you?" He replied, "I am Ali bin Abi Talib." The Jew cried out, "O assembly of Jews, your defeat has arrived!" At this moment, Marhab the Jew, the commander of that stronghold, came to the battlefield to fight Ali عليه السلام, and it was not long before he fell to the ground with one effective blow.

A fierce battle broke out between the Muslims and the Jews. Ali عليه السلام came near the gate of the fortress and, with a powerful and mighty movement, tore the gate from its place and cast it aside. Thus, the fortress was opened, and the Muslims entered and conquered it.

The Jews surrendered and asked the Prophet that their lives be spared in exchange for this surrender. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله accepted; the movable spoils fell to the hands of the Islamic army, and he entrusted the lands and orchards there to the Jews on the condition that they pay half of the income to the Muslims 1.

1: "Kamil ibn al-Athir" (the famous Sunni historian), Vol. 2, pp. 216-221 (summarized).

Ayahs

وَلَوْ قَـٰتَلَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوَلَّوُا۟ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (٢٢)
If the faithless fight you, they will turn their backs [to flee]. Then they will not find any protector or helper. (22)
سُنَّةَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ ٱللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا (٢٣)
[It is] Allah’s precedent that has passed before, and you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent. (23)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ عَنْهُم بِبَطْنِ مَكَّةَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَنْ أَظْفَرَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا (٢٤)
It is He who withheld their hands from you, and your hands from them, in the valley of Makkah, after He had given you victory over them, and Allah sees best what you do. (24)
هُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَٱلْهَدْىَ مَعْكُوفًا أَن يَبْلُغَ مَحِلَّهُۥ ۚ وَلَوْلَا رِجَالٌ مُّؤْمِنُونَ وَنِسَآءٌ مُّؤْمِنَـٰتٌ لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوهُمْ أَن تَطَـُٔوهُمْ فَتُصِيبَكُم مِّنْهُم مَّعَرَّةٌۢ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۖ لِّيُدْخِلَ ٱللَّهُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا۟ لَعَذَّبْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (٢٥)
They are the ones who disbelieved and barred you from the Sacred Mosque, and kept the offering from reaching its destination. And were it not for [certain] faithful men and faithful women, whom you did not know—lest you should trample them, and thus the blame for [killing] them should fall on you unawares; [He held you back] so that Allah may admit into His mercy whomever He wishes. Had they been separate, We would have surely punished the faithless among them with a painful punishment. (25)

Commentary

If War Had Broken Out at Hudaybiyyah

These verses continue to recount other dimensions of the great event of "Hudaybiyyah" and point to two important matters in this regard.

First: Do not imagine that if a conflict had occurred between you and the polytheists of Makkah in the land of "Hudaybiyyah," the polytheists would have won the war; it is not so. "And if those who disbelieve had fought you, they would have turned their backs [in flight]. Then they would not find a protector or a helper" (وَلَوْ قَاتَلَكُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوَلَّوُا الْأَدْبَارَ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا).

And this is not exclusive to you: "[This is] the established way of Allah which has occurred before. And never will you find in the way of Allah any change" (سُنَّةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ اللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا).

This is an eternal divine law: if the believers do not show weakness or lethargy in the matter of Jihad, and rise to struggle against the enemies with a pure heart and sincere intention, Allah will grant them victory. There may sometimes be delays or advancements in this matter for the purpose of testing or other objectives, but the ultimate victory is certainly theirs.

However, in instances such as the field of "Uhud," where a group disobeyed the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and another group tainted their intentions with the love of the world and engaged in gathering spoils, a bitter defeat eventually overtook them; and it shall be the same hereafter.

An important point pursued by these verses is that the Quraysh should not sit back and say, "Alas, that we did not rise up and crush this small group! Alas, that the prey came to our house and we were negligent of it! Alas and alas!" Not at all; although the Muslims were few compared to them, far from home and sanctuary, and lacking sufficient weapons, nevertheless, had a conflict occurred, victory would still have belonged to them by the blessing of the power of faith and divine assistance. Were their numbers not fewer and the enemy's equipment greater at "Badr" or "Ahzab"? How then did defeat overtake the enemy in both cases?

In any case, stating this reality served to strengthen the morale of the believers, weaken the morale of the enemies, and put an end to the "ifs" and "buts" of the hypocrites. It showed that even under seemingly unequal conditions, if a battle occurs, victory belongs to the sincere believers!

Another point clarified in these verses is where He says: "And He it is who withheld their hands from you and your hands from them within [the region of] Makkah after He caused you to overcome them. And Allah is ever Seeing of what you do" (وَهُوَ الَّذِي كَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنْكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ عَنْهُمْ بِبَطْنِ مَكَّةَ مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ أَظْفَرَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا).

Truly, this event was a clear manifestation of "Manifest Victory" (فتح المبين), the very description the Quran chose for it. A limited group without sufficient military equipment enters the enemy's land—an enemy that had repeatedly campaigned against Medina and made extraordinary efforts to crush them—yet now that they have stepped into his city and territory, he is so intimidated that he proposes peace. What victory is greater than achieving such superiority over the enemy without even a drop of blood being shed from anyone's nose?! Undoubtedly, the event of the Peace of Hudaybiyyah was considered a defeat for the Quraysh throughout the Arabian Peninsula and a victory for the Muslims, as they had been able to intimidate the enemy to such an extent.

A group of commentators have mentioned a "Reason for Revelation" (Sha'n al-Nuzul) for this verse, which is:

During the event of Hudaybiyyah, the polytheists of Makkah secretly mobilized forty men to strike the Muslims, but through the vigilance of the Muslims, their plot was foiled. The Muslims captured them all and brought them to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله released them.

Some have recorded their number as 80 men who wanted to ambush the Muslims from Mount "Tan'im" during the morning prayer, utilizing the darkness.

Others have said that while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was sitting in the shade of a tree to draft the peace treaty with the representative of the Quraysh, and Ali عليه السلام was busy writing, 30 young men from Makkah attacked him with weapons. Their plot was miraculously neutralized, they were all captured, and the Prophet freed them [1]. According to this "Reason for Revelation," the phrase "after He caused you to overcome them" (مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ أَظْفَرَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ) refers to the victory over this specific group, whereas according to the previous interpretation, it refers to the general victory of the army of Islam over all the polytheists, and this is more consistent with the content of the verse.

It is noteworthy that the Quran emphasizes the absence of conflict "within Makkah." This expression may point to two things: First, Makkah was the center of the enemy's power, and normally they should have used this opportune moment to attack the Muslims; as the saying goes, they were searching for the Muslims in the sky, and when they caught them on their own ground, they should not have let them go easily—but Allah took away their power! Second, Makkah was the secure Sanctuary (Haram) of Allah; if conflict and bloodshed had occurred there, on one hand, the sanctity of the Haram would have been violated, and on the other hand, it would have been considered a disgrace for the Muslims, as if they had broken the traditional security of this holy land. Therefore, one of Allah's great blessings upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims was that Makkah was conquered two years after this event, and that too was without bloodshed.

In the last verse under discussion, another point regarding the issue of the Peace of Hudaybiyyah and its philosophy is mentioned:

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 123. This Reason for Revelation has also been narrated with slight differences by "Qurtubi," "Abu al-Futuh Razi," "Alusi" in "Ruh al-Ma'ani," "Sheikh Tusi" in "Tibyan," "Maraghi," and others.

He says: "They [your enemies] are the ones who disbelieved and barred you from the Sacred Mosque and [prevented] the offering from reaching its place of sacrifice" (هُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ وَالْهَدْيَ مَعْكُوفًا أَنْ يَبْلُغَ مَحِلَّهُ) [1]. One of their sins was their disbelief, and another sin was that they barred you from the rites of Umrah and the circumambulation of the House of Allah, and did not allow the sacrificial camels to be sacrificed at their destination, namely Makkah (the place of sacrifice for Umrah is Makkah, and for Hajj, it is the land of Mina). This is while the House of Allah must be free for all people of faith, and preventing it is among the greatest sins, as the Quran says elsewhere:

"And who is more unjust than he who prevents the name of Allah from being mentioned in His mosques...?" (Baqarah: 114). These sins necessitated that Allah punish them at your hands and penalize them severely.

But why did He not do so? The end of the verse clarifies the reason, saying: "And if not for believing men and believing women whom you did not know—that you might have trampled them and there would have befallen you because of them a disgrace without [your] knowledge—[Allah would have permitted you to enter]" (وَلَوْلَا رِجَالٌ مُؤْمِنُونَ وَنِسَاءٌ مُؤْمِنَاتٌ لَمْ تَعْلَمُوهُمْ أَنْ تَطَؤُهُمْ فَتُصِيبَكُمْ مِنْهُمْ مَعَرَّةٌ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ) [2].

[1]: "Ma'kufan" is from the root "Akuf," meaning to be prevented from movement and staying in one place.

[2]: The answer to "Lawla" (if not for) in the above sentence is omitted, and its implied meaning is: "He would not have withheld your hands from them"—or—"You would have trampled the necks of the polytheists by Our helping you."

This verse refers to a group of Muslim men and women who had embraced Islam but were unable to migrate for certain reasons and remained in Makkah.

If the Muslims had attacked Makkah, the lives of this group of oppressed Muslims in Makkah would have been endangered, and the tongues of the polytheists would have been loosened, saying that the army of Islam shows mercy neither to its opponents nor even to its followers and supporters; and this would have been a great disgrace.

Some have also said that this "disgrace" refers to the necessity of expiation (kaffarah) and blood money (diyah) for accidental killing, but the first meaning seems more appropriate.

"Ma'arrah" is from the root "Arr" (rhyming with Sharr) and "Urr" (rhyming with Hurr), originally meaning the disease of scabies, a type of severe skin condition that affects humans or animals; later it was expanded to apply to any kind of harm or loss that reaches a person.

Then, to complete this statement, He adds: "[This was] so that Allah might admit into His mercy whom He wills" (لِيُدْخِلَ اللَّهُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ مَنْ يَشَاءُ).

Yes, Allah willed that the oppressed believers of "Makkah" be included in His mercy and that no harm should reach them.

The possibility has also been raised that one goal of the "Peace of Hudaybiyyah" was for a group of polytheists who were capable of being guided to be guided and enter the mercy of Allah.

The expression "whom He wills" (مَنْ يَشَاءُ) refers to those who possess the merit and worthiness, for the Divine Will always stems from His Wisdom, and the Wise One does not intend without a reason, nor does He perform an action without calculation.

And at the end of the verse, for further emphasis, He adds: "If they had been apart [from them], We would have punished those who disbelieved among them with a painful punishment" (لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا لَعَذَّبْنَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا).

It is true that Allah could have separated this group from others through a miracle, but the Sunnah (way) of the Lord, except in exceptional cases, is to perform tasks through natural means.

"Tazayyalu" is from the root "Zawal," meaning here to become separate and dispersed.

From numerous narrations cited by both Shi'a and Sunni sources under this verse, it is understood that it refers to the faithful individuals who were in the loins of the disbelievers; Allah did not punish this group of disbelievers for their sake.

Among these, we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Someone asked the Imam عليه السلام: Was Ali عليه السلام not strong and powerful in the religion of Allah? The Imam عليه السلام said: Yes, he was strong. He asked: Then why did he gain mastery over certain peoples (of faithless individuals and hypocrites) but did not eliminate them? What prevented him?

He said: A verse in the Holy Quran! He asked: Which verse?

He said: This verse where Allah says: 'If they had been apart, We would have punished those who disbelieved among them with a painful punishment.'

Then he added: 'Indeed, Allah, Mighty and Majestic, had believing deposits in the loins of a people who were disbelievers and hypocrites, and Ali عليه السلام was not one to kill the fathers until the deposits had come forth! ... And likewise, our Qa'im of the Ahl al-Bayt will never appear until the deposits of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, have appeared!'" [1]

Meaning that Allah knows that a group of their children will accept faith in the future by their own will and choice, and for their sake, He exempts the fathers from immediate punishment.

"Qurtubi" has mentioned this meaning in other words in his commentary.

There is no obstacle to the above verse referring both to the mixing of the believers of Makkah with the disbelievers and to the believers who were in their loins.

[1]: "Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 70. Numerous other narrations have also been recorded in this regard.

Ayah

إِذْ جَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْحَمِيَّةَ حَمِيَّةَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ فَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ وَعَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ وَكَانُوٓا۟ أَحَقَّ بِهَا وَأَهْلَهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا (٢٦)
When the faithless nourished bigotry in their hearts, the bigotry of pagan ignorance, Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and the faithful, and made them abide by the word of Godwariness, for they were the worthiest of it and deserved it, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (26)

Commentary

Ignorant Prejudice and Zealotry—The Greatest Barrier for the Disbelievers!

In these verses, the issues related to the event of "Hudaybiyyah" continue to be followed, depicting other scenes of this great historical occurrence.

First, it points to one of the most significant factors preventing the disbelievers from believing in Allah and the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and from submitting to truth and justice, saying: "Remember when those who disbelieved harbored in their hearts disdain and the zealotry of [the days of] ignorance" (إِذْ جَعَلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْحَمِيَّةَ حَمِيَّةَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ) ^1

Because of this, they prevented the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the believers from entering the House of Allah and performing the rites of Umrah and sacrifice. They said: "If these people, who killed our fathers and brothers on the battlefield, enter our land and homes and return safely, what will the Arabs say about us? What credit and prestige would remain for us?"

This pride, arrogance, prejudice, and ignorant rage even prevented them—during the drafting of the "Hudaybiyyah" peace treaty—from allowing the name of Allah to be written as "In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful" (Bismillahir-Rahmanir-Rahim), despite the fact that their own customs and traditions dictated that visiting the House of Allah was permitted for everyone and that the land of Mecca was a sanctuary of peace. Even if someone saw the killer of his own father in that land or during the Hajj and Umrah rituals, he would not disturb him.

By this act, they violated the sanctity of the House of Allah and His secure sanctuary, trampled upon their own traditions, and drew a thick veil between themselves and the truth. Such are the deadly effects of "ignorant zealotry" (Hamiyyat al-Jahiliyyah)! "Hamiyyah" originally comes from the root Hama (حمى), meaning the heat generated by fire, the sun, or the human body. For this reason, the state of "fever" is called Humma (حمى), and the state of anger, as well as pride and "wrathful prejudice," is called Hamiyyah.

This is a state that arises from ignorance, narrow-mindedness, and cultural decadence, especially prevalent among "ignorant nations," and it becomes the source of many of their wars and bloodshed.

Then it adds: In contrast to that, "Allah sent down His tranquility (Sakina) upon His Messenger and upon the believers" (فَأَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى‌ رَسُولِهِ وَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

This tranquility, which was born of faith and belief in Allah and reliance upon His grace, called them to composure and self-control. It extinguished the fire of their anger to the extent that, to preserve their great objectives, they were willing to remove the phrase "In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful"—which was the symbol of Islam in starting affairs—and instead write "In Your name, O Allah" (Bismika Allahumma), which was a remnant from the past era of the Arabs, at the beginning of the Hudaybiyyah treaty. They even agreed to remove the title "Messenger of Allah" from beside the noble name of "Muhammad" (peace be upon him and his family).

They were willing, contrary to their burning love and desire to visit the House of Allah and perform the Umrah rituals, to return to Medina from Hudaybiyyah itself, to sacrifice their camels right there—contrary to the usual custom of Hajj and Umrah—and to exit the state of Ihram without performing the rites.

Yes, they were willing to "bite their tongues" and exercise patience and endurance in the face of all these hardships. Had "ignorant zealotry" ruled over them, any one of these instances would have been enough to ignite the flames of war in 그 land.

Indeed, the culture of ignorance calls for "zealotry," "prejudice," and "ignorant rage," but the culture of Islam calls for "tranquility," "peace," and "self-control."

Then it adds: "And He made them stick to the word of righteousness (Taqwa), and they were most worthy of it and deserving of it" (وَ أَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ التَّقْوى‌ وَ كانُوا أَحَقَّ بِها وَ أَهْلَها).

"Word" (Kalimah) here means "spirit"; that is, Allah cast the spirit of Taqwa into their hearts and made it their constant companion. Just as we read in Surah An-Nisa, verse 171, regarding Jesus (peace be upon him): "The Messiah, Jesus, son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him" (إِنَّمَا الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ وَ كَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى‌ مَرْيَمَ وَ رُوحٌ مِنْهُ‌).

Some have also suggested that "the word of Taqwa" refers to the command and decree that Allah had given to the believers in this regard.

However, it is more appropriate to interpret it as the "spirit of Taqwa" which has a "creative" (takwini) aspect and is born of faith, tranquility, and heart-felt commitment to Allah's commands.

Therefore, in some narrations quoted from the noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), "the word of Taqwa" is interpreted as "There is no god but Allah" (La ilaha illa Allah) ^1, and in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), it is interpreted as "Faith" (Iman) [^2]. In one of the sermons of the noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), we read:

"We are the word of Taqwa and the path of guidance." (نحن كلمة التقوى و سبيل الهدى‌) [^3]

A similar meaning is narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Ridha (peace be upon him), who said:

"And we are the word of Taqwa and the firmest handhold." (و نحن كلمة التقوى و العروة الوثقى‌) [^4]

It is clear that faith in "Prophethood" and "Wilayah" complements faith in the principle of "Monotheism" (Tawhid) and the knowledge of Allah, for they are all callers to Allah and heralds of Monotheism.

In any case, the Muslims in these sensitive moments did not fall prey to rage, nervousness, prejudice, and pride. They did not burn the brilliant destiny that Allah had ordained for them in the event of Hudaybiyyah with the fire of anger and ignorance.

For He says: "The Muslims were most worthy of Taqwa and were its people and its place."

^1: Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 80. [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 73. [^3]: Al-Khisal by Saduq; Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 73. [^4]: Same source, p. 74.

Naturally, from a group of superstitious, ignorant, and idol-worshipping people, nothing but "ignorant zealotry" was expected. But from monotheistic Muslims who had been nurtured for long years in the school of the Quran, such ignorant character traits were unexpected. What was expected of them was that very tranquility, dignity, and Taqwa which they displayed at Hudaybiyyah. Although some impatient and hot-tempered individuals—who perhaps still carried residues from the past—were close to breaking this powerful barrier and causing a commotion, the tranquility and dignity of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) was poured like water upon this fire and extinguished it.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah has always been All-Knowing of all things" (وَ كانَ اللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيماً).

He knows both the evil intentions of the disbelievers and the pure-heartedness of the true believers. Here He sends down tranquility and Taqwa, and there He allows ignorant zealotry to prevail, for Allah grants His grace and mercy—or His wrath and anger—to every nation and people according to their merits!

Key Point: What is Ignorant Zealotry?

We mentioned that "Hamiyyah" originally comes from the root Hama, meaning heat, and was later used to mean anger, and subsequently to mean pride and prejudice mixed with anger.

This term is sometimes used in this blameworthy sense (coupled with the qualifier "of ignorance" or without it) and sometimes in a praiseworthy and commendable sense, referring to logical zeal and commitment toward positive and constructive matters.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), while criticizing some of his weak-willed and rebellious companions, says:

"I am afflicted with people who do not obey when I command, and do not respond when I call... Is there no religion to unite you? Or no zeal (Hamiyyah) to stir you?" (and drive you to fulfill your duties) ^1

However, it is mostly used in the blameworthy sense, as the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), emphasized many times in the "Qasi'ah" sermon. In condemning Iblis (Satan), who was the leader of the arrogant, he says:

"The children of pride and zealotry, the brothers of fanaticism, and the riders of the mount of arrogance and ignorance followed him." [^2]

In another part of the same sermon, while warning people against the prejudices of ignorance, he says:

"Extinguish the fires of fanaticism and the grudges of ignorance that are hidden in your hearts, for this pride, zealotry, and improper prejudice in a Muslim are from the promptings, arrogance, whispers, and incitements of Satan!" [^3]

In any case, there is no doubt that the existence of such a state in an individual or a society causes the backwardness and downfall of that society. It casts heavy veils over human reason and thought.

It prevents one from correct understanding and sound judgment, and sometimes blows all of one's interests to the wind.

Fundamentally, the transfer of wrong traditions from one people to another takes place under the ominous shadow of this "ignorant zealotry," and the persistence of deviant nations against the Prophets and divine leaders is also often due to this.

In a tradition from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him), we read that when he was asked about "fanaticism" (Asabiyyah), he said:

"The fanaticism for which its possessor is sinful is that a man considers the wicked of his own people to be better than the good of another people. It is not fanaticism for a man to love his own people, but fanaticism is to help his people in oppression." ^1

The best way to combat this ugly trait and the way to escape this great peril is to strive to raise the level of culture, thought, and faith of every nation and group.

In truth, the Holy Quran has stated the remedy for this pain in this very verse under discussion, where, in contrast to it, it discusses the believers who possess tranquility and the spirit of Taqwa. Therefore, where there is faith, tranquility, and Taqwa, there is no ignorant zealotry; and where there is ignorant zealotry, there is no faith, tranquility, or Taqwa!

^1: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 39. [^2] and [^3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 192 (Sermon of al-Qasi'ah). ^1: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 73 (Hadith 70).

Ayah

لَّقَدْ صَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ رَسُولَهُ ٱلرُّءْيَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ لَتَدْخُلُنَّ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ ٱلْحَرَامَ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ءَامِنِينَ مُحَلِّقِينَ رُءُوسَكُمْ وَمُقَصِّرِينَ لَا تَخَافُونَ ۖ فَعَلِمَ مَا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا۟ فَجَعَلَ مِن دُونِ ذَٰلِكَ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا (٢٧)
Certainly Allah has fulfilled His Apostle’s vision in all truth: You will surely enter the Sacred Mosque, God willing, in safety and without any fear, with your heads shaven or hair cropped. So He knew what you did not know, and He assigned [you] besides that a victory near at hand. (27)

Commentary

The True Vision of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)

This verse depicts another significant episode from the events of "Hudaybiyyah." The story was as follows:

While in Medina, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) saw a dream in which he and his companions entered Mecca to perform the rituals of the "Umrah." He shared this dream with his companions, and they were all filled with joy. However, since some imagined that the interpretation and fulfillment of this dream would occur that very same year, they fell into doubt and hesitation when the polytheists blocked their path to Mecca at Hudaybiyyah. They wondered: Is it possible for the Prophet's vision to turn out incorrect? Were we not supposed to be honored with visiting the House of Allah? What happened to that promise? And where did that divine dream go?! In response to these questions, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Did I tell you that this vision would be fulfilled this very year?"

The above verse was revealed regarding this matter on the way back to Medina, emphasizing that this was indeed a true vision and that its fulfillment was certain, definitive, and bound to happen.

It states: "Allah has certainly fulfilled His Messenger's vision in truth" (لَقَدْ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ رَسُولَهُ الرُّؤْيا بِالْحَقِ‌) [1]. Then it adds: "You shall certainly enter the Sacred Mosque, if Allah wills, in security, with your heads shaved or your hair shortened, having no fear" (لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ آمِنِينَ مُحَلِّقِينَ رُؤُسَكُمْ وَ مُقَصِّرِينَ لا تَخافُونَ‌).

"But He knew what you did not know" (فعلم ما لم تعلموا).

And there was wisdom in this delay, "and He granted, besides that, a near victory" (فَجَعَلَ مِنْ دُونِ ذلِكَ فَتْحاً قَرِيباً).

[1]: "صَدَقَ" (Sadaqa) is a past tense verb that sometimes takes two objects, as in the above verse where "رسوله" (His Messenger) is the first object and "رؤيا" (vision) is the second. Occasionally, its second object is preceded by "فى" (in), such as "صدقته فى حديثه" (I found him truthful in his speech).

Several points in this verse draw attention

  1. Considering that the "Lām" in "لَتَدْخُلُنَّ" (Latadkhulunna) is the "Lām of Oath" and the "Nūn" at the end is for emphasis, this is a definitive divine promise regarding the future. It is an explicit, miraculous prophecy of performing the Umrah rituals in total security. As we shall mention, this prophecy became a reality exactly the following year, in the same month of Dhu al-Qi'dah, and the Muslims performed the Umrah rituals in this very manner.
  1. The phrase "إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ‌" (if Allah wills) here may be a form of instruction to the servants so that they do not forget to rely on the divine Will and Providence when speaking of the future, and not to consider themselves independent in their affairs or without need of His grace. It may also refer to the conditions Allah set for this success (the blessing of visiting the House of Allah in the near future), which is remaining on the path of Monotheism, tranquility (Sakina), and piety. Furthermore, it may refer to individuals whose lives would end during this interval and who would not succeed in performing this pilgrimage. It is entirely possible to combine all these meanings.
  1. According to many commentators, the expression "فَتْحاً قَرِيباً" (a near victory) refers to the "Peace of Hudaybiyyah," which the Quran calls a "Manifest Victory" (فتح مبين). We know that this very victory paved the way for entering the Sacred Mosque the following year. However, another group considers it a reference to the "Conquest of Khaybar." Of course, the word "قريبا" (near) is more consistent with the Conquest of Khaybar, as there was a shorter interval before the actual realization of this dream. Moreover, in verse 18 of this same Surah, which speaks of the "Pledge of Ridwan," it says: "He sent down tranquility upon them and rewarded them with a near victory" (فَأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ أَثابَهُمْ فَتْحاً قَرِيباً). As we mentioned and as most commentators believe, this refers to the "Conquest of Khaybar." The evidence within the verse also suggests this, and since the verse under discussion is harmonized with that one, it seems both refer to the same meaning [1]. This meaning is also mentioned in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim [2].
  1. The phrase "مُحَلِّقِينَ رُؤُسَكُمْ وَ مُقَصِّرِينَ‌" (with your heads shaved or your hair shortened) refers to one of the rituals of Umrah called "Taqsir," by which the pilgrim exits the state of Ihram. Some have taken this verse as evidence for "choice" (Takhyir) in the matter of Taqsir and exiting Ihram, meaning the pilgrim can either shave the head or shorten the hair (or nails), as combining both is certainly not obligatory.
  1. The phrase "فَعَلِمَ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا" (He knew what you did not know) refers to the important secrets hidden within the "Peace of Hudaybiyyah" that were revealed over time. The foundations of Islam were strengthened, the fame of Islam spread everywhere, accusations of warmongering against Muslims were dispelled, and the Muslims were able to conquer Khaybar with peace of mind. They could send their missionaries to the corners of the Arabian Peninsula, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) could send his historic letters to the great leaders of the world at that time—matters that ordinary people were unaware of and only Allah knew.
  1. In this verse, we encounter the issue of "Vision" (Ru'ya)—the true vision of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), which is a branch of revelation, similar to what occurred regarding Ibrahim (عليه السلام) and the sacrifice of his son Ismail (عليه السلام) (As-Saffat, verse 102). (For more details on visions and dreams, see Volume 9, in the story of Yusuf, page 312 onwards).
  1. The verse under discussion is one of the reports of the unseen (Ghayb) in the Quran, a testament to the divine origin of this Book, and one of the miracles of the noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). With such decisiveness and emphasis, it foretells both the entry into the Sacred Mosque to perform Umrah in the near future and a near victory before that. As we know, both prophecies came to pass. You have already heard the story of the Conquest of Khaybar; now hear the story of "Umrat al-Qada."

Umrat al-Qada

"Umrat al-Qada" is the Umrah that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) performed the year after Hudaybiyyah, in Dhu al-Qi'dah of the 7th year of the Hijra (exactly one year after the polytheists had barred them from entering the Sacred Mosque), accompanied by his companions. It was named so because it was considered the "repayment" (Qada) of the previous year.

To explain: according to one of the articles of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, the plan was for the Muslims to freely perform the Umrah and visit the House of Allah the following year, but they were not to stay in Mecca for more than three days. During this period, the leaders of Quraysh and the prominent polytheists of Mecca were to leave the city (both to avoid potential conflict and because those who, out of malice and prejudice, could not bear to see the sight of the Muslims' monotheistic worship, would not have to witness it!).

It is recorded in some histories that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his companions entered the state of Ihram and set out with sacrificial camels until they reached near "Zahran." At this point, the Prophet sent one of his companions named "Muhammad ibn Maslamah" ahead with a significant number of horses and weapons. When the polytheists saw this, they were deeply afraid and thought the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) intended to fight them and violate the ten-year treaty.
