They informed the people of Mecca, but when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) reached near Mecca, he ordered that the arrows, spears, and other weapons be moved to a land called "Yajaj," and he and his companions entered Mecca with only swords, and those were sheathed.

When the people of Mecca saw this act, they were pleased that the promise was kept (it seems the Prophet's action was a warning to the polytheists that if they intended to break the covenant or plot against the Muslims, the Muslims had the power to confront them).

The leaders of Mecca left the city so as not to see these sights, which were heartbreaking for them, but the rest of the people of Mecca—men, women, and children—gathered along the path, on rooftops, and around the House of Allah to see the Muslims and their Umrah rituals.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) entered Mecca with a special grandeur, accompanied by many sacrificial camels. He treated the people of Mecca with the utmost kindness and courtesy. He ordered the Muslims to move quickly during the circumambulation (Tawaf) and to adjust their Ihram garments slightly to reveal their strong and broad shoulders, so that this scene would leave an impression on the minds of the Meccans as a living proof of the strength, power, and bravery of the Muslims.

Altogether, "Umrat al-Qada" was both an act of worship and a display of power. It must be said that the seeds of the "Conquest of Mecca," which occurred the following year, were sown during those days, fully preparing the ground for the Meccans' submission to Islam.

This situation was so unpleasant for the leaders of Quraysh that after three days had passed, they sent someone to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) stating that according to the agreement, he must leave Mecca as soon as possible.

Interestingly, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) married a widow from the women of Mecca who was related to some of the famous leaders of Quraysh, in order to strengthen his ties with them according to Arab custom and to reduce their enmity and opposition.

When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) heard the proposal to leave Mecca, he said: "I would like to prepare a meal for this marriage ceremony and invite you"—an act which, if performed, would have had a significant impact on further influencing their hearts. However, they did not accept and officially rejected this invitation [1].

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan" by Tabarsi, Vol. 9, p. 127; "Fi Zilal al-Quran," Vol. 7, p. 511; and "Tarikh al-Tabari," Vol. 2, p. 310 (summarized and synthesized).

[1 (from point 3)]: The expression "مِنْ دُونِ ذلِكَ‌" (besides that) either means "before that"—meaning before the Umrah of the following year, Allah would grant the believers a near victory—or it means "other than that"—meaning that besides the success of visiting the House of Allah, He also granted them a near victory.

[2 (from point 3)]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 76.

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا (٢٨)
It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion, that He may make it prevail over all religions, and Allah suffices as witness. (28)
مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥٓ أَشِدَّآءُ عَلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ رُحَمَآءُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ تَرَىٰهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنًا ۖ سِيمَاهُمْ فِى وُجُوهِهِم مِّنْ أَثَرِ ٱلسُّجُودِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ ۚ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِى ٱلْإِنجِيلِ كَزَرْعٍ أَخْرَجَ شَطْـَٔهُۥ فَـَٔازَرَهُۥ فَٱسْتَغْلَظَ فَٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَىٰ سُوقِهِۦ يُعْجِبُ ٱلزُّرَّاعَ لِيَغِيظَ بِهِمُ ٱلْكُفَّارَ ۗ وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِنْهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًۢا (٢٩)
Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in worship], seeking Allah’s grace, and [His] pleasure. Their mark is [visible] on their faces, from the effect of prostration. Such is their description in the Torah and their description in the Evangel. Like a tillage that sends out its shoots and builds them up, and they grow stout and settle on their stalks, impressing the sowers, so that He may enrage the faithless by them. Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward. (29)

Commentary

Severe Against Enemies and Merciful Among Friends!

In these two verses, which are the final verses of Surah al-Fath, reference is made to two other important matters in connection with the "Manifest Victory" (the Peace of Hudaybiyyah). One concerns the global spread of Islam, and the other describes the qualities and characteristics of the companions of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and recounts the divine promise regarding them.

First, He says: "He is the One who sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth, to make it prevail over all religions, and Allah is sufficient as a witness to this" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى‌ وَ دِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً).

This is an explicit and decisive promise from Allah, the Almighty, the All-Powerful, regarding the victory of Islam over all religions.

That is to say, if Allah informed you through the vision of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of the victory—that you would enter the Sacred Mosque (Masjid al-Haram) in complete security and perform the Umrah rituals without anyone daring to disturb you—and if Allah gives the glad tidings of a "near victory" (the conquest of Khaybar), do not be surprised. These are just the beginning; eventually, Islam will become global and will prevail over all religions.

Why should it not be so, when the content of the Messenger of Allah's call is guidance (arsalahu bil-huda) and His faith is truth (wa din al-haqq)? Any impartial observer can see its truthfulness in the verses of this Quran, and in the individual, social, judicial, and political laws of Islam, as well as its moral and human teachings. Furthermore, through the precise and explicit prophecies it makes about the future—which come to pass exactly—one can certainly know the connection of this Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to Allah.

Yes, the powerful logic of Islam and its rich, fruitful content necessitate that it eventually sweeps away polytheistic religions, humbles distorted heavenly religions before it, and attracts hearts toward this pure faith through its deep magnetism.

Regarding whether this victory refers to a "logical victory" or a "military victory," there is discussion among the commentators:

A "group" believes this victory is solely a "logical and argumentative victory," and this has already been achieved, as Islam is superior to all existing faiths in terms of the power of logic and reasoning.

Meanwhile, "another group" has taken victory to mean "external dominance" and the triumph of power. The usage of this word (yuzhirahu) also points toward external dominance. For this reason, it can be said: in addition to the vast regions in the East, West, North, and South of the world that are currently within the realm of Islam—with more than 40 Islamic countries and a population of about one billion under the banner of Islam—a time will come when the entire world will officially come under this banner. This matter will be completed through the uprising of "al-Mahdi" (may our souls be sacrificed for him), as narrated in a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله who said:

"There shall not remain upon the face of the earth a house of stone and clay, nor a tent of fur and hair, but that Allah shall cause the word of Islam to enter it!" [^1]

We had a detailed discussion on this subject under the commentary of a similar verse, Verse 33 of Surah al-Tawbah. [^2] It is also noteworthy that some have considered the expression "al-Huda" (the Guidance) as a reference to the firmness of Islamic "beliefs," while they view "Din al-Haqq" (the Religion of Truth) as referring to the truthfulness of the "branches of religion" (furu' al-din). However, we have no evidence for this division; it appears that both guidance and truthfulness exist in both the principles (usul) and the branches (furu').

Regarding whether the pronoun in "to make it prevail" (liyuzhirahu) refers to "Islam" or "the Prophet" صلى الله عليه وآله, commentators have offered two possibilities. However, the context clearly shows that it refers to the religion of truth, as it is grammatically closer to the pronoun, and the victory of a religion over other religions is more appropriate than a person over a religion.

The final word regarding the verse is that the phrase "And Allah is sufficient as a witness" (kafa billahi shahida) points to the reality that this prophecy needs no other witness or testimony, for its witness is Allah. Likewise, the messengership of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله needs no other witness, as Allah is its witness. If Suhayl ibn Amr and his ilk are not willing to write the title "Messenger of Allah" after the name of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, "they only bring shame upon themselves and cause us no trouble!"

In the final verse, a very vivid portrayal of the companions and special followers of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—those who were on his path—is presented from the tongue of the Torah and the Gospel. This is both a source of pride and glory for those who showed steadfastness at Hudaybiyyah and other stages, and an instructive lesson for all Muslims throughout all centuries and ages.

At the beginning, He says: "Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah" (مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ‌).

Whether "bats" like Suhayl ibn Amr like it or not? Whether they hide themselves from this world-illuminating sun or not? Allah has testified to His messengership, and all those who are aware testify to it.

Then, He proceeds to describe his companions, detailing their outward and inward qualities, their emotions, thoughts, and actions through five characteristics: "Those who are with him are severe and firm against the disbelievers" (وَ الَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ).

In the second description, He says: "But merciful among themselves" (رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ).

Yes, they are a center of affection and love toward their brothers, friends, and co-religionists, but a fierce, burning fire and a firm, steely barrier against enemies.

In truth, their emotions are summarized in this "mercy" and "wrath." However, neither does the combination of these two in their existence create a contradiction, nor does their wrath against the enemy or their mercy toward the friend cause them to step away from the path of truth and justice.

In the third characteristic, which speaks of their actions, He adds: "You see them bowing and prostrating, constantly engaged in the worship of Allah" (تَراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً).

This expression depicts the worship and servitude of Allah through its two main pillars, "bowing" (ruku') and "prostration" (sujud), mentioning it as their permanent and constant state—a worship that is the secret of submission to the command of Truth and the negation of pride, selfishness, and arrogance from their being.

In the fourth description, which discusses their pure and sincere intention, He says: "They seek bounty from Allah and His pleasure" (يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رِضْواناً).

They do not take steps for show or hypocrisy, nor do they expect reward from Allah's creation; rather, their eyes are fixed only on His pleasure and bounty. This is the sole motivation for their movement throughout life.

Even the use of the term "bounty" (fadl) shows that they admit their shortcomings and consider their deeds too insignificant to demand a divine reward; rather, despite all their effort and striving, they still say: "O Allah! If Your bounty does not come to our aid, woe unto us!"

In the fifth and final description, He discusses their refined and luminous appearance, saying: "Their mark is on their faces from the trace of prostration" (سِيماهُمْ فِي وُجُوهِهِمْ مِنْ أَثَرِ السُّجُودِ). ^3

"Sima" originally means a mark or appearance, whether this mark is on the face or another part of the body, although in everyday Persian usage, it refers to the features of the face and the outward appearance of the countenance.

In another sense, their "physiognomy" clearly shows that they are humans humble before Allah, the Truth, the law, and justice. This mark is reflected not only in their faces but in their entire being and life.

Although some commentators have interpreted it as the physical mark of prostration on the forehead, or the trace of dust from the place of prostration, the verse seemingly has a broader meaning that fully portrays the countenance of these men of God.

Some have also said: This verse refers to their place of prostration on the Day of Resurrection, which will shine like the moon when it is full! Of course, it is possible that their foreheads will be like this in the Hereafter, but the verse informs us of their outward state in this world.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned regarding the interpretation of this phrase: "It is staying awake at night for prayer" (the effects of which appear on their faces during the day). [^4] Certainly, combining these meanings is entirely possible.

In any case, after stating all these qualities, the Quran adds: "This is their description (the companions of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله) in the Torah" (ذلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ). This is a reality that was spoken of previously and a description in a great heavenly book revealed more than a thousand years prior.

However, it must not be forgotten that the expression "And those who are with him" (wa-lladhina ma'ahu) speaks of those who were with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in everything—in thought, belief, ethics, and action—not just those who were contemporary with him while their path differed from his.

Then, He proceeds to describe them in another great heavenly book, the Gospel (Injil), saying: "Their description in the Gospel is like a seed which sends forth its blade, then strengthens it, so it becomes thick and stands upon its own stem, growing and becoming so fruitful that it fills the sowers with wonder" (وَ مَثَلُهمْ فِي الْإِنْجِيلِ كَزَرْعٍ أَخْرَجَ شَطْأَهُ‌ فَآزَرَهُ فَاسْتَغْلَظَ فَاسْتَوى‌ عَلى‌ سُوقِهِ يُعْجِبُ الزُّرَّاعَ‌). [^5]

"Shat'a" means "shoot" or "sprout"—the sprouts that come out from the bottom of the stalk and beside the roots. "Azara" is from the root muwazarah, meaning assistance. "Istaghladha" is from the root ghildhah, meaning to become firm and thick. The phrase "stood upon its stem" (astawa 'ala suqihi) means it became so firm that it stood on its own (note that suq is the plural of saq, meaning stem/leg).

The expression "fills the sowers with wonder" (yu'jibu al-zurra') means it has reached such a level of rapid growth, numerous sprouts, and abundant harvest that even the farmers, who are constantly dealing with these matters, are filled with amazement.

Interestingly, in the second description found in the Gospel, five major qualities are also mentioned for the believers and companions of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله: (sprouting – assisting in growth – becoming firm – standing on its own – remarkable and amazing growth).

In truth, the qualities mentioned for them in the Torah are those that describe the dimensions of their existence in terms of emotions, goals, actions, and outward appearance. However, the qualities in the Gospel describe their movement, growth, and development in various aspects (observe closely).

Yes, they are humans with lofty attributes who do not stop "moving" for a moment; they are constantly sprouting, and the sprouts are nurtured and become fruitful. They constantly spread Islam through their words and deeds in the world, and day by day, they add new throngs to the Islamic society.

Indeed, they never sit idle and are constantly moving forward. While being worshippers, they are mujahids (strivers), and while in jihad, they are worshippers. They have a refined exterior, a purified interior, powerful emotions, and pure intentions. Against the enemies of Truth, they are the manifestation of Allah's wrath, and toward the friends of Truth, they represent His grace and mercy.

Then, in the continuation of the verse, He adds: These lofty qualities, this rapid growth, and this blessed movement, just as they bring joy and vigor to friends, cause anger to the disbelievers: "That He may enrage the disbelievers with them" (لِيَغِيظَ بِهِمُ الْكُفَّارَ). [^6]

At the end of the verse, He says: "Allah has promised those among them who believe and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward" (وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَ أَجْراً عَظِيماً).

It is obvious that the qualities mentioned at the beginning of the verse already included faith and righteous deeds; therefore, the repetition of these two qualities points to their continuity. That is, Allah has given this promise only to that group of Muhammad's صلى الله عليه وآله companions who remain on his path and continue their faith and righteous deeds. Otherwise, those who were among his friends and companions one day, but separated from him the next and took a path contrary to it, are never included in such a promise.

The expression "among them" (minhum)—considering that the word min in such cases is primarily for "partition" (tab'id), and the literal meaning of the verse conveys this—is evidence that his companions will be divided into two groups: a group that continues in faith and righteous deeds and becomes subject to Allah's vast mercy and great reward, and a group that separates and will be deprived of this great grace.

It is not clear why a group of commentators insists that min in minhum in the above verse must be "explicative" (bayaniyyah). Even if we were to go against the literal meaning and take min as explicative, how can we set aside the rational evidence that exists here? For no one claims that all the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were infallible (ma'sum). In that case, the possibility of not continuing on the path of faith and righteous deeds exists for each of them. Given this, how could Allah give the promise of forgiveness and a great reward unconditionally to all of them, whether they follow the path of faith and righteousness or turn back halfway and deviate?

It is also noteworthy that the phrase "those who are with him" (wa-lladhina ma'ahu) does not mean merely being a contemporary or having physical companionship with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, for the hypocrites also had such companionship. Rather, "with him" certainly means being together in terms of the principles of faith and piety.

Therefore, we can never derive a general ruling from the above verse regarding all contemporaries and associates of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Key Points

1- The Story of the Sanctification of the Companions!

It is well-known among the scholars and thinkers of the Ahl al-Sunnah that the companions (Sahaba) of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله possess a special distinction over other members of the Ummah: that they are all pure and untainted, far from pollution, and we have no right to criticize any of them. Speaking ill of them is absolutely forbidden, and according to some, it even leads to disbelief! To prove this aim, they have relied on verses from the Holy Quran, including the verse under discussion which says: "Allah has promised those among them who believe and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward."

They also rely on "Verse 100 of Surah al-Tawbah," which, after mentioning the "Muhajirun" and "Ansar," says: "Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him" (رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَ رَضُوا عَنْهُ‌).

However, whenever we free ourselves from preconceptions, clear evidence stands before us that destabilizes this famous belief:

1- The phrase "Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him" in Surah al-Tawbah is not exclusive to the Muhajirun and Ansar, for in the same verse, alongside the Muhajirun and Ansar, are "those who followed them in goodness" (alladhina-ttaba'uhum bi-ihsan), a concept that includes all those who follow them in goodness until the Day of Resurrection.

Just as the "followers," if they are on the path of faith and goodness one day and on the path of disbelief and wrongdoing (isa'ah) the next, exit from under the umbrella of divine pleasure, the same applies to the "companions." For in the final verse of Surah al-Fath, He conditioned them upon faith and righteous deeds; if this title is stripped from them one day, they will exit the circle of divine pleasure.

In other words, the expression "in goodness" (bi-ihsan) applies to both the "followers" and the "followed." Therefore, whichever of the two abandons the "path of goodness" will not be subject to Allah's pleasure.

2- It is understood from Islamic narrations that although the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had the distinction of his companionship, those who come in later eras and possess firm faith and righteous deeds are, from one perspective, superior to the companions. This is because the companions witnessed various miracles, whereas others have stepped onto the same path without seeing them, using other proofs.

As we read in a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that his companions asked: "Are we your brothers, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "No, you are my companions; my brothers are those who come after me, who believe in me though they have not seen me." Then he added: "The one among them who acts righteously has the reward of fifty of you!" They said: "Fifty of them, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "No, fifty of you!" They repeated this three times (and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله denied it), then he said: "This is because you find helpers for good deeds (whereas they do not)." [^7]

In Sahih Muslim, it is also narrated from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله that one day he said: "I wish that we could see our brothers!" They said: "Are we not your brothers, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "You are my companions, but our brothers are those who have not yet come!" [^8]

Reason and logic also say that others who were not under the cover of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله continuous instruction day and night, yet possessed faith and righteous deeds equal to or greater than the companions, are superior.

3- This claim is also very vulnerable historically, as we see some companions who went astray after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or even during his own time.

How can we absolve of sin those who ignited the fire of the Battle of the Camel, caused the death of so many Muslims, and drew swords against the rightful Caliph of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله? Or those who gathered at Siffin and Nahrawan and rose in rebellion against the successor and vicegerent of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the choice of the Muslims, shedding countless blood—how can we consider them subject to Allah's pleasure and say that the dust of disobedience never touched their skirts?

Even stranger is the excuse of those who justify all these oppositions by claiming they were "Mujtahids" and a Mujtahid is excused! If such great sins can be justified through "Ijtihad," then no killer can ever be blamed, nor can divine punishments be carried out against him, for he might have performed Ijtihad.

In other words, on the fields of the Camel, Siffin, or Nahrawan, two groups stood against each other, and certainly both were not on the truth, for the combination of opposites is impossible. Given this, how can both be considered subject to Allah's pleasure, especially since the matter was not so complex or difficult that it could not be discerned? For everyone knew that Ali عليه السلام was his rightful successor, either by the explicit text of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or by the choice of the Muslims. Despite this, they drew swords against him; how can this act be justified through Ijtihad? Why do they not justify the rebellion of the "Ahl al-Riddah" (People of Apostasy) during the time of Abu Bakr through Ijtihad and officially consider them apostates, yet consider the rebels of the Camel, Siffin, and Nahrawan free from any sin?

In any case, it seems that the issue of the absolute sanctification of the companions was a political ruling that a group emphasized after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to preserve their own position and protect themselves from any criticism. This is something that accords neither with the judgment of reason nor with established Islamic histories; it is a "rhyme that will trap us in its meter."

It is far better that, while respecting the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and those who were always on his path, we consider the criteria for judging them to be their actions and beliefs throughout their lives from beginning to end—the same criteria we have derived from the Quran and the same criteria with which the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself measured his companions.

2- Mutual Islamic Love

In the Islamic narrations regarding the interpretation of the final verse, great emphasis is seen on the principle of "merciful among themselves" (ruhamau baynahum).

Among these, we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "A Muslim is the brother of a Muslim; he does not wrong him, he does not desert him, and he does not intimidate him. It is incumbent upon Muslims to strive in connecting, cooperating in affection, and consoling those in need, being kind to one another, so that you may be as Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, commanded you: 'merciful among yourselves,' showing mercy to one another, being concerned for the affairs of those absent from you, following what the assembly of the Ansar practiced during the time of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله." [^9]

But it is strange that today's Muslims have distanced themselves from the effective guidance of this verse and the characteristics mentioned for true believers and companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. Sometimes they fall upon one another with such malice and bloodshed that the enemies of Islam never did! Sometimes they form such bonds of friendship with disbelievers as if they were brothers of the same lineage.

There is no sign of that bowing and prostration, nor those pure intentions and "seeking the bounty of Allah," nor the marks of prostration visible on the faces, nor that growth, development, sprouting, strengthening, and standing on one's own feet.

And the wonder is that the more we have distanced ourselves from these Quranic principles, the more we have been afflicted with pain, suffering, humiliation, and misery; yet we still do not realize from where we are being struck! Still, "the zealotry of ignorance" (hamiyyat al-jahiliyyah) prevents reflection, reconsideration, and a return to the Quran. O Allah, wake us from this deep and dangerous sleep.

O Allah! Grant us the success to revive within ourselves the moral characteristics of the true companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his sincere associates mentioned in these verses.

O Lord! Grant us firmness against enemies, love toward friends, submission to Your command, attention to Your special favors, and the effort and striving to make the Islamic society fruitful, and to advance and expand it.

O Lord! We ask You for a manifest victory under whose shadow our Islamic society may move forward, and in an age and time when the need for spirituality is greater than ever, let us present the teachings of this life-giving faith to the people of the world, conquering new hearts for Islam every day, and opening a new country from the realm of hearts (Amen, O Lord of the Worlds).

End of Surah al-Fath 6 Shawwal 1405 / June 25, 1985
